Preface
This thesis has been prepared over five months at the Section for Design and Processes, De­
partment of Civil and Mechanical Engineering, at the Technical University of Denmark, DTU,
in partial fulfilment for the degree in Master of Science in Engineering – Civil Engineering,
M.Sc. Eng. The extent of the thesis is 30 ECTS points.
It is assumed that the reader has a basic knowledge in the areas of fire safety and fire
dynamics.
Karoline Sandfeld­Nielsen ­ s173793

ii
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
21/01/2023
==PAGE:5==
Abstract
The aim of this thesis was to determine a safety distance between wooden facades on high­
rise building, to prevent fire propagation. To investigate and determine a safety distance the
following are covered and completed in this report.
Two methods were used as preliminary hand calculations to determine a safety distance.
Rough FDS models were created to support the results, and determine which method gave
the most realistic results. For the hand calculations and rough FDS models, it was assumed
that the whole facade was ablaze and radiating toward an adjacent facade.
Experimental testing was executed with four intermediate facade burning experiments. Each
of the four experiments were influenced by varying exterior conditions, such as various wind
speeds and size in ignition source. The construction of the facade was stationary and was
2.4 m wide and 1.9 m high. The greatest temperature was measured with thermocouple type
K, to be 888.4°C in the cavity. Additionally bomb calorimetry tests of the wooden species
used in the facade construction were conducted. All the wooden species were pinewood but
treated differently by the suppliers.
A FDS model of the first facade burning test was created. This model did not include a
wind source, and the ignition source in FDS was on 14 kW, instead of the 9.5 kW used
in the experiment. The FDS model’s results fitted somewhat to the physical experiment’s
results. The largest difference in model and physical test, was the flame propagation. The
propagation of flames in the FDS and physical experiment was similar up until the ignition
source was removed. The flames extinguished when the ignition source was removed in the
physical experiment, where in the FDS model the flames continued. This might be credited
to the model not accounting for Ts,min. The temperature development in the FDS model, was
like the experiment test, up until the ignition source was removed.
The FDS model of the intermediate facade burning experiment was scaled up to resemble
two wooden facades adjacent to one another, where on one of the facades a fire was simu­
lated. Six different cases were created. Where there were two fire scenarios. One scenario
had a fire comparable to a waste basket with a HRR on 100 kW. The other fire was coming
from two windows in a compartment, which had experienced a flashover. There were three
different distances tested for each initial fire, which where 5 m, 7.5m, and 10 m. The fire on
the burning facade was too small to influence an adjacent facade with radiation significant.
From the simulations it was found that 5 m would be a safe distance, but this was due to the
initial fire not being large, and not spreading very much. Furthermore, the simulations did
not run for more than 888 seconds. Whereas it was found with the initial rough models, that
when the whole facade was ablaze, a safety distance on 12.2 m would be sufficient to not
spread fire to an adjacent building.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
iii
==PAGE:6==
Resumé
Formålet med dette kandidatspeciale var at bestemme en sikkerhedsafstand mellem to høj­
huse med træfacader således, at der ikke vil opstå brandspredning. For at undersøge og
bestemme en sikkerhedsafstand, er følgende blevet udført og gennemgået i denne rapport:
Der blev udført indledende håndberegninger, hvor to metoder blev brugt, til at udregne en
sikkerhedsafstand. Yderligere blev der lavet grove FDS modeller, med tilhørende simu­
leringer, til at støtte op om håndberegningerne, og undersøge hvilken metode der gav de
mest realistiske resultater. Det var antaget i både håndberegningerne og FDS modellerne,
at hele den brandramte facade var antændt.
Fire eksperimentelle forsøg blev udført, med facadeafbrændinger under forskellige omkring­
værende forhold. Hvert afbrændingsforsøg blev udsat for varierende kombinationer af vin­
dstyrker og størrelse på brændstofkilde. Selve facadekonstruktionen blev der ikke ændret
på undervejs. Facadekonstruktionen var 2,4 m bred og 1,9 m høj. Den højeste temper­
atur målt, med termokobler type K, lå på 888,4°C, hvilket var inde i det ventilerede hulrum.
Yderligere blev der udført bombekalorimetri på de forskellige typer af træ, brugt til at bygge
facaden op. Alle trætyperne var fyrretræ, men de var fra producentens side blevet behandlet
på forskellige måder.
Der blev udført en FDS model af det første facadeafbrændingsforsøg. I denne model var
der ikke nogen vindpåvirkning på facaden, og der blev benyttet en brandkilde på 14 kW,
selvom der i forsøget blev brugt en brandkilde på 9,5 kW. Resultaterne fra FDSsimulerin­
gen var sammenlignelige med det fysiske forsøg op indtil brandkilden blev fjernet. Inden
brandkilden blev fjernet, havde både simulering og forsøg samme temperatur­ og flamme­
spredningsforløb. Efter brandkilden blev fjernet i forsøget døde flammerne ud kort tid efter.
I FDS simuleringen fortsatte flammespredningen, og dermed var der også målt højere tem­
peraturer senere i brandforløbet i FDS simuleringen sammenlignet med forsøget. Dette kan
forklares med, at der ikke er blevet taget højde for Ts,min i FDS modellen.
FDS­modellen af facadeafbrændingsforsøget, blev skaleret op til at ligne to højhuse med
træfacader placeret over for hinanden. Der var på den ene af facaderne placeret en brand,
der skulle sprede sig til resten af facaden. Der blev lavet seks forskellige modeller. Der
blev brugt to brandscenarier, det ene scenarie blev kodet til at minde om en affaldsspand
med en brandeffekt på 100 kW. Det andet brandscenarie var kodet til at være flammer fra
to vinduer i facaden. Fra hvert vindue var der en brandeffekt på 200 kW. For hver af de
to brandscenarier, blev der modelleret tre forskellige afstande på hhv. 5 m, 7,5 m og 10
m. Selve branden på facaden i simuleringerne spredte sig ikke særlig meget, og var derfor
forholdsvis lille. Branden var så lille, at denne ikke havde nogen betydelig effekt på den
modstående facade. Det var således muligt at udlede, at der ikke ville ske brandspredning
mellem facaderne, med en afstand helt ned på 5 m. Dog er dette kun gældende for de første
888 sekunder af brandforløbet, og for lige netop de brandscenarier og den facadeopbygning
brugt i denne rapport. Resultatet fra de grove modeller, hvor hele facaden var antændt, var,
at der skulle være en sikkerhedsafstand på minimum 12,2 m for at undgå brandspredning
mellem træfacaderne.
iv
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings

Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:13==
1
Introduction
1.1
Introduction and Motivation
The increased focus on the building sector’s carbon footprint has created a need to build
more environmentally sustainable. This has led to changes in the Danish building legislation
from 2023. From 2023 there will be requirements for production of buildings materials and
waste processing of building materials by the end of their usage. There is an enhanced focus
on new buildings’ life cycles [1]. Wood as a building material is much less CO2 demanding,
than the traditional building materials such as concrete and steel. Hence wood is used more
often in new constructions, not only in small single­family houses, but in big high­risers and
prestige projects. E.g. Mjøstårnet, an 85.4 m high­rise building in Norway [2], the new UN17
village, with several apartment blocks in Copenhagen [3] and Marmormolen, a domicile in
Copenhagen [4]. Some of these new buildings are a mix between wood, concrete, and
steel, but often the aim is to build with as much wood as possible. Today wood can be used
in the structural parts, room dividers and facades [2]. However, buildings with wood in bigger
projects, such as high risers, are challenging in regard to the fire safety regulations, and the
legislation can inhibit the use of wood [5].
To aid in the process of building more sustainable with wood, there is a need to investigate
how to construct fire safe wooden buildings. Today’s focus, regarding fire safety, is fire spread
on wooden facades, and how it spreads on facades. However, investigations of fire spread
between high­rise buildings with wooden facades are limited.
Fire spread between buildings, especially in densely built cities, can be critical, since it can
lead to large city fires, this has been seen before in Japan, Denmark, and Germany, see
section 2 ­ Urban­ and Facade Fires for more. If a whole city burns down, it is of little value if
it was built environmentally sustainable in wood, because all the stored CO2, will be released
back into the atmosphere. Therefore, it is crucial to construct new buildings with great caution
and be aware of the fire risks, and which measurements are needed to prevent fire spread.
1.2
Aim and Objective
There has been an increase in facade fires in recent years [6], and on top of that, there is an
enhanced pressure on the construction industry to apply more wood [7]. The occurrence of
facade fires and the added amount of combustible material can be a dangerous combination
for personal and property safety, if not handled with caution. A great amount of research has
been completed regarding facade fires in wood. Most research have focused fire spread on
a singular facade, which is also of great importance. However, there is less research in the
safety distance between wooden facades. Therefore, the aim of this thesis is to investigate:
What safety distance, between high­rise buildings with wooden facades, should be applied
to avoid fire propagation?
This is to be accomplished with a combination of preliminary hand calculations, experiments
and CFD simulations.
1.3
Limitations
For a fire to spread between buildings, the fire needs to be huge and cover a large section
of the facade. Large flames must be present. This demands facades which contains a great
amount of combustible material, that can be used as fuel. Then the heat flux and radiation
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
1
==PAGE:14==
might be large enough to reach and ignite a radiation exposed facade. To reach this type
of fire spread and growth, it will have to be late in the duration of the fire. Therefore, rather
large, but realistic, fires and fuel sources are applied in this thesis. To furthermore narrow
the scope of the thesis, a few limitations are applied:
• The focus is on high­rise buildings, hence legislation and fires in smaller buildings are
not included. Additionally in this project a building is regarded as a high­rise building
when it is above 12 m (∼4 floors).
• Only one type of wooden facade is considered. The facade is constructed in a specific
way, and there is only used pine wood. A thorough description of the facade is given
in section 4.1.1. There are many ways to construct a wooden facade, and different
wooden species can be used, hence the results from this thesis applies only for this
specific facade construction. The results cannot be generalized to all wooden facades.
• The wind’s effect on fires varies depending on wind direction and wind speed. For this
reason, there is the assumption in the CFD simulations that no wind is present.
1.4
Method
1.4.1
Background Information
Literature with background information has been written, to emphasize why this thesis is rel­
evant, and bring background information to the reader regarding legislation, how a common
wooden facade is built, how many high­rise facade fires have occurred in recent years, and
the severity of them, and history on urban fires. The primary sources for the literature used
have been articles found by using DTU’s digital library Findit and Google Scholar, where
access to articles has been granted through DTU.
1.4.2
Experiments
Experiments are a part of this thesis. Four tests with a wooden facade fire in FireLab, with
different conditions were performed. The facade burning experiments were completed in
collaboration with Joakim Wåhlén and built with the help of Morten Mølgaard Schmidt.
Experiments with a Bomb Calorimeter have been run on the wooden materials used in the
facade. Testing of the wooden materials was made to calibrate the FDS model, with heat of
combustion data, to be more attuned to the facade experiment.
1.4.3
Preliminary Calculations
Preliminary hand calculations were done to find a safety distance, between two wooden
facades. Two methods have been used, one by the SFPE Handbook, chapter 86, and one by
theory used on radiation from smoke and flames, from the book: Temperature Calculation in
Fire Safety Engineering, by Ulf Wickström. In addition to the calculations, rough FDS models
have been made to support the calculations.
1.4.4
CFD Simulations
Multiple CFD simulations have been conducted using The National Institute of Standards
and Technology’s (NIST) simulation software FDS (Fire Dynamic Simulator), additionally the
simulations have been run on Central DTU HPC Cluster (LSF 10). A FDS model have been
made to resemble the first facade burning experiment. In this model the results from the
bomb calorimeter test were utilized. The results from the FDS simulation have been com­
pared to the results from the experiment. The FDS model was then scaled up to resemble
two high­rise buildings with wooden facades. Both buildings were modelled to be 17 m tall
and 15 m wide. Six simulations with variations of this model were run, with different distances
between the facades, and two different fire scenarios.
2
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:15==
2
Background Information
2.1
Legislation Regarding Wooden Facades and Safety Distance
This section describes the Danish legislation regarding building facades, and the safety dis­
tance between buildings. Since this report focus on high­rise buildings, the requirements
described are for high­rise buildings, however there are some overlaps between regulation
for smaller buildings.
According to the Danish building legislation § 82, part 4, when a building is designed, it must
be designed in a way so, in case of fire, fire will not spread to other buildings. Furthermore,
according to § 125, a building must be placed, so fire will not spread to other buildings [8].
2.1.1
Prescriptive-Based Codes
The prescriptive codes from the Danish building legislation offers two different distance re­
quirements. One is the distance between buildings on the same plot, and the other is the
distance from the building to the neighboring site boundary.
In table 2.1 there is an overview of the prescriptive codes’ safety distances regarding place­
ment of buildings near site boundary and buildings on the same plot. As it can be observed
in table 2.1 the distance differs depending on the construction of the external wall surface
and which material classification the rain cover has.
Table 2.1: Overview of the relation between covering class, material class and distance.
Adapted from BR18 chapter 5, chapter 4 – Ignition, fire­ and smoke propagation [9]

Further specifications of table 2.1:
1. External wall surfaces, on buildings with top floor being 22 m above ground or less, can
be in covering class K1 10 / B­s2,d0, instead of K1 10 / B­s1,d0. This can be constructed
in such a way, that either the outer wall is in covering class K1 10 / B­s2,d0, without
a rain cover, or with rain cover being in B­s2,d0, and the underlying wall surface is in
covering class K1 10 / B­s1,d0.
2. Rain cover in material class B­s1,d0, is only allowed to be used in buildings with top
floor being 22 m above ground or less. If the top floor is placed more than 22 m above
ground, the rain cover has to be in material class A2­s1,d0.
3. If a building is fully sprinkled, the distance to site boundary can be decreased to 2.5 m.
Furthermore, if a building is placed less than 2.5 meters from the site boundary, the outer
wall facing the site boundary must be constructed in building part class REI 120 A2­s1, d0.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
3
==PAGE:16==
If there are any installations or other building parts that are part of the wall facing the site
boundary, they have be constructed in a way, so it does not decrease the fire resistance of
the wall [9, section 4.7.1].
Facades in material class D­s2,d2 is only allowed on buildings, which is either one story tall
or top floor is maximum placed 5.1 meters above ground, if the building is fully sprinklered
[9].
2.1.2
Performance-Based Codes
There is the option to use performance­based codes, when designing new buildings. This is
used when it is not possible to follow the prescriptive codes. When it comes the performance­
based codes regarding safety distance between buildings, the acceptance requirement is;
there must not be a thermal heat radiation on the adjacent building on more than 15 kW/m²
within the first 60 minutes of the beginning of the fire. This is described in the Danish building
regulation chapter 5 – Fire, chapter 8 ­ Verification Guide [10, section 8.2.4].
2.2
Construction of Wooden Facades in Commercial Use
It is important to notice the difference between rain cover and facade cladding. The primary
purpose of a rain cover is to protect, the materials behind it, against the weather. Where
the objective of the facade cladding is to protect the materials behind it against fire. This is
the reason why there are different requirement for respectively coverings class and material
class [11].
Construction of an external wall with wooden facade, is in the commercial world often done
in one of the following three different ways:
A The external wall is constructed using a wooden frame, this can either be with or without
primary load bearing elements. Then the facade cladding is suspended on the exterior
side.
B The external wall is constructed with a load bearing rear wall, which can be in concrete
or masonry. In front of the rear wall a wooden frame (front wall) is placed, where the
facade cladding is suspended, just as if the whole construction was in wood.
C The facade cladding can also be suspended on a heavy external wall, where both front
and rear wall are in concrete or masonry.
There are in figure 2.1 three drawings of the different exterior walls described above. The
figure is a redrawn version of the one found in TRÆ 55 [11]. A facade cladding can also
be suspended on and used with prefabricated wooden elements. Usually the prefabricated
wooden element producers’ offer special­made elements, that fit the costumers’ need. Here
the facade cladding can either be part of the prefabricated exterior wall element or be sus­
pended on the element later in the construction process.
It is worth mentioning, that steel is also used in construction today, and can be used in a
frame structure, in the same fashion as a wooden frame, described above in point A.
4
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:17==
Figure 2.1: Examples of external walls with wooden facade cladding
2.2.1
Void and Ventilation
Generally, there are four different ways of suspending a wooden facade suspended on the ex­
ternal wall in regard to ventilation and void between the exterior wall and the facade cladding
[12]:
A The void can be fully rear ventilated. There is a void between the external wall and the
facade cladding, furthermore there are openings in the top and bottom of the wall, so
it is possible to have an air flow between the external wall and the facade cladding.
B The void can partly rear­ventilated. Having the same construction as mentioned in A,
but there is only a vent­opening in the bottom of the construction.
C The void can be non­ventilated. Meaning there is still a void between external wall and
cladding, but there are no ventilation openings. Therefore the air in the void is standing
still, besides for the movement due to buoyancy.
D The cladding can be directly suspended on the external wall, leaving no room for a void
and ventilation.
A visual overview of the suspending with and without a void can be seen in figure 2.2.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
5
==PAGE:18==
Figure 2.2: Four types of voids behind a facade
2.2.2
Suspending Method
The most common used wooden facade cladding methods are either overlapping or tongue
and groove. When using the overlapping of boards method, the overlapping must be at least
22 mm due to fire safety reasons. However, when built in the real world the overlapping will
most likely be at least 25 mm due the moist avoidance measurements. The facade boards
used in the facade cladding can be suspended horizontal or vertical. The boards in the facade
cladding are suspended on counter battens. When the boards are placed in the horizontal
direction, they are suspended on vertical counter battens. When the facade boards are
positioned in the vertical direction, they are suspended on horizontal counter battens, and
these are suspended on vertical counter battens [11]. If the suspending system, the counter
battens, are constructed in wood and there are both horizontal and vertical battens, more
than 10 percent of the wood will be exposed, which is opposed to the requirement in the
building regulations. If either the horizontal or vertical wooden battens are changed to metal,
then the suspending system will be legal. The suspension system just described is only
allowed for buildings with top floor placed 22 m above ground or lower [9, section 4.4.10].
The suspending methods are visually presented in figure 2.3
6
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:19==
Figure 2.3: Suspending types: A & B: Horizontal and vertical battens. C & D: Only vertical battens.
A & C: tongue and groove. B & D: Overlapping
2.2.3
Commercially Used Wooden Species
The most commonly wooden species used in Denmark are Douglass pine, Western red
cedar, spruce, oak and larch. These wood species are found and produced in the northern
European forests [11]. In addition to the use of wooden planks as facade cladding, other
wooden products, that have been through a process, can be used as paneling. The wooden
products generally used are; hardboard, medium density fiberboard (MDF), oriented strand­
board (OSB), particleboard, plywood and waferboard [13]. Wooden boards used as facade
cladding, need to be CE­marked in accordance to EN 14915 [11]. Most wood based facades
can be categorized as covering class K1 10 D­s2,d2, if not tested [11].
2.3
Fire Performance of Wood
There is a great difference between wooden species. Wood can be divided into two cate­
gories: Softwood and hardwood. The thermal properties of each of the wooden species add
to the difference in the burning of it as a fuel. As a general rule hardwood, e.g., beech and
birch, is denser than softwood, e.g., pine and fir [14], [15].
Wooden products usually reach the European Reaction to Fire Class D­s1, d0[16] or D­s2,
d0 and Dfl­s1 for flooring [17]. It is not uncommon, for wood products to be placed in class
D­s2,d2 [10]. Wood is an organic material of different species, and can be treated in different
ways, hence the fire rating class, depending on the wooden product, can vary greatly.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
7
==PAGE:20==
2.3.1
Temperature and Flaming
Temperatures from experimental wooden facade fires, have been examined, and the follow­
ing have been observed. From a paper during full scale testing on wooden facades, a facade
with unpainted wood panels, was tested with the SP fire 105 test. From this test a tempera­
ture peak was measured to 950°C on the facade [16]. Another experimental study of facade
fires, measured on the burning facade a temperature between 800­900°C. This facade was
constructed with birch wood [18].
In the SFPE Handbook, chapter 86, the author of that chapter remarks, that the lowest ex­
pected flame temperature on a wooden facade would be 1100K (827°C) [19, Chapter 86]. If
a wooden facade panel is thin enough to be thermally thin, a fire can continue, whereas if
the wooden panel is so thick, that it is thermally thick, the fire is most likely to self­extinguish
[20].
2.3.2
HRR and Heat Flux
The heat release rate and heat flux from wood depends on several different factors, such
as the influence from the environment, how much wood is available and which wood specie
is applied. Heat fluxes for different wood species calculated based on the flames ranges
from 23.9 kW/m² to 77.5 kW/m² [20]. Furthermore an intermediate facade fire burning test,
with painted pine wood boards showed a HRR which reached ∼300 kW after 15 minutes of
burning [21]. When burning wood pallets stacked 3 m high a HRR on 7 MW is generated
[22].
2.3.3
Heat of Combustion
There are complete heat of combustion and effective heat of combustion. The complete
heat of combustion, ΔHc, is the total amount of heat release, for a quantity oxidized under
ideal conditions, which is at 25°C and atmospheric pressure [23]. In this thesis, when heat
of combustion is mentioned, it is the complete heat of combustion, which is referred to. The
effective heat of combustion, ΔHc,eff, is the ratio between the heat release rate and the mass
loss rate [23]. Heat of combustion values for different wooden species are given in table 2.2.
Table 2.2: Complete heat of combustion values for wooden species [19, SFPE Handbook
table 5.3, 26.33 and A.38]
Wood specie
Heat of Combustion [MJ/kg]

Douglas fir

Pine
17.9
European beech

European beech char

2.4
Urban- and Facade Fires
2.4.1
Urban Fires
Numerous cities have experienced a city fire. Some cities have even been rebuilt and burned
down again, due to a new city fire. The concept of city fires is not new, to mention one of the
earliest, but yet very significant city fire is The Great Fire of Rome (year 64). The Great Fire
of Rome lasted 6 days, and ruined more than 70 % of the city [24]. When rebuilding the city,
8
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:21==
emperor Neo enforced building regulations regarding fire safety. Fireproof materials should
be used in external walls [25]. Another impactful city fire is The Great Fire of London in 1666,
where more than 80 % of the city was destroyed. This resulted in the first building regulations
in London, requiring houses to be built with stone and brick, and have fire resisting walls in
separations [26].
The city of Copenhagen has burned down three times respectively in 1728, 1795 and 1807.
In 1728 the fire started in a residential building, and burned most of the inner city down. The
city fire lasted for three days, before the fire department put out the fire. As a consequence
of the fire, some streets were straightened out, new buildings’ street facing wall had to be
built of stone or brick and not constructed in timber framing, however that requirement got
renounced [27]. The concept of “Ildebrandshus” (Eng: Fire House) was introduced and used
in some new buildings. The Fire House was be a building in two to three stories, with gable
dormers in masonry, the external wall facing the street would be constructed in stone/brick
and the rest of the building in a timber frame [28].
Figure 2.4: A general sketch of a fire house from 1744 [27].
In 1795 the second Great Fire of Copenhagen started in the navy’s lumberyard by Gammel­
holm. Here the fire grew so ferociously, that it could “jump” over Holmens Canal (a water
filled canal back in 1795): The fire spread to all of the southern part of the old city, and the fire
continued for two days, before it was extinguished. After the fire, the requirement regarding
a mason wall facing the street, was reinforced, and the corners of new buildings had to be
beveled. The requirement of beveled corners was to make is easier for the fire department
to get through the city and reach the fire faster [29]. It was also decided, that not all of the city
should be rebuilt, thus creating more space between buildings [30]. In 1807 from 2nd ­ 4th of
September, Copenhagen was bombarded by England. This bombardment is considered the
first terror bombardment of a European big city. Yet again the southern part of Copenhagen
burned down [31].
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
9
==PAGE:22==
In the 20th century large city fires occurred in Hamburg, Dresden, Tokyo and Hiroshima.
Those fires where due to several air­raids during the second world war. The bombing of
Dresden resulted in a firestorm, destroying almost 85% of the city, equaling 15 km². Likewise,
the bombing of Hiroshima lead to a firestorm, which spread in a radius over 4 km. The worst
fire bombing was in Tokyo, where the bombing and fire destroyed 40 km² of the city and killing
around 84.000 people [32]. It has been observed that when firestorms occur, temperature
exceeds 1000°C in the center of the fire [33].
In recent years fires like the Edinburgh Cowgate fire (2002) and The Camp Fire (2018) have
made it clear that city fires can still occur today. The Cow Gate fire was a smaller city fire,
which occurred in a densely built area of Edinburgh, with historical and old buildings. It took
the fire brigade three days to put out the fire.The fire started in a night club and spread to
the surrounding buildings [34]. The Camp Fire was a wildfire moving into an urban area, and
destroyed more than 18.800 structures (“structures” included the following: Single family
houses, mobile homes, businesses, detached garages, outbuildings and larger sheds > 120
ft²) in the areas of Paradise and Concow in California [34].
Causes of City Fires
There are several ways a city fire can start. The first great fire of Copenhagen is believed
to have started by an accident. A boy dropping a candle in hay, and then first the building
was on fire, which spread to other buildings [35]. However that is years ago, and not likely
to happen again. Today a city fire will most likely start in one of the following ways; As an
act of war or terror, where bombing or arson is a way to purposely destroy, nature disasters,
such as earthquakes, which can cause many fires to start at the same time, and wildfires,
which can move from vegetation into urban areas. With several fires spread all over the city,
emergency services can face great difficulties in extinguishing all the fires, before they get
out of hand [33], [36], [37].
Consequences of Urban Fires
For a long period of time, the common buildings materials in Denmark have been steel,
concrete, and masonry. This is due to experiences with former city fires, both within Denmark
and foreign countries. The regulations in use today, requires that the building can withstand
a fire long enough for occupants to evacuate, and rescue personnel to provide assistance.
Furthermore there are required distances between buildings. Those requirements transpired
from prior city fires, and experiences with fires over time. In the past buildings, constructed
in wood, had to be placed far from each other. Now wood is used in larger constructions and
buildings again, and brought back into the cities [33].
Even though the fire safety in buildings today has improved, there have been implemented
heavy fire regulations, and the used of open fires in the everyday life has decreased, city
and building fires can still occur today. There will still be fire safety challenges. Wooden
constructions are back in the cities again. Accidents like in Cowgate can still happen. Terror,
war and wildfire can also cause a threat to the safety of the cities if the building industry
forgets the hard learned experiences from the past. The building industry needs to build in
a way, that in case of an accident the buildings do not contribute to a city fire, but instead
prevent the spread of fire [33].
2.4.2
High-Rise Facade Fires
Houses with five to seven stories in France were already seen in the seventeenth century,
but it was first in the late nineteenth century, that tall buildings with ten or more stories were
constructed. Thus the occurrence of facade fires in high­rise buildings are in a historic per­
spective relatively new [38]. The first recorded facade fire in a high­rise building was in
10
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:23==
Canada in 1990, which is more than a hundred years after the first tall building was built.
Research shows that there have been 59 facade fire incidents from 1990 to 2018, where
most of the facade fires have happened after 2000. The facade fires that have occurred,
have been in recently constructed high risers or in newly refurbished high­rise buildings [6].
An example of this is the Grenfell tower case, which was constructed in 1972­1974 and re­
furbished in 2015­2016. Since 2018 (to writing moment 16/01­2023) 15 facade fires have
occurred [39], with the most recent fire in a 35 story residential building in Dubai on the 7th
of November 2022.
Most of the oldest high­rise buildings from the late nineteenth century were constructed in
masonry and steel, and later concrete [40]. Combustible exterior wall systems are commonly
used in newer high­rise buildings from the twenty­first century [41].
Recent high­rise facade fires are described below:
Grenfell Tower ­ 2017
In 2017 a fire broke out in The Grenfell Tower, a 24­story tall high­rise building in London.
According to an article in Journal of Hazardous Materials 368 [42], the fire started inside a
compartment in a fridge/freezer on fourth floor. The fire spread to the exterior of the building,
and then spread rapidly to most of the facade, and into almost all the apartments above [42].
In 2006 UK relaxed the requirements regarding the use of combustible materials on the ex­
terior of buildings and increased the requirements for the energy efficiency of buildings. Due
to this change in regulations, it was then allowed to use ACM­PE (Aluminum Composite Ma­
terial ­ Polyethylene) cladding system in the facade, when Grenfell Tower renovated it. In
2016 the refurbishing of Grenfell was done, and the new insulated facade was suspended
on the existing concrete exterior wall. The new facade consisted of combustible polyisocya­
nurate (PIR) foam insulation and aluminum­polyethylene composite material, separated by
a ventilated cavity. Furthermore, new windows were fitted [42]. Investigations by an arti­
cle in Journal of Hazardous Materials 368 [42] identifies PE in the ACM­PE panel to have
contributed to the rapid fire spread.
The Marina Torch ­ 2015 and 2017
The Marina Torch is a 300­meter­tall high­rise building with 86 stories in Dubai. The high­
rise’s facade has been on fire twice once in 2015 and again in 2017. The facade was con­
structed with an ACM­PE based cladding system, just as the system used in the refurbish­
ment of The Grenfell Tower [43]. The facade was also rebuilt with an ACM­PE based cladding
system.
The first fire started on a balcony on the 50th floor, and spread to the exterior of the facade
[44]. There was a rapid fire spread due to high winds, and ignited debris falling to the ground.
The facade fire spread from the 50th floor to the top [43]. The second fire started on the 26th
floor, and is reported to have started due to cigarette butts being discarded into a potted
plant. Yet again the fire spread to the top floor [44]. The fire spread rapidly due to falling
flaming debris, which started a second fire in the bottom of the tower [43]. The building is
sprinklered, which resulted in fire damage being contained to only the exterior of the building,
and no reported casualties in both fires [44].
China Telecom Building ­ 2022
One of the most recent facade fires in a high­rise building is the fire in the China Telecom
building, which broke out on the 16th of September. The fire was discovered about 15.47 in
the 218­meter­tall building. The fire spread was rapid, and in about ten minutes the whole
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
11
==PAGE:24==
front side of the facade, covering 42 floors, was covered in flames and smoke. The 280
firefighters were able to extinguish the rapid fire, and had it put out by 16.30. According to
an article by Global Times, no casualties have been reported [45]. The fire was reported to
have started in an external air condition unit, and then glass from a few windows burst, and
fell to the ground [46].
Figure 2.5: Facade fires in relatively Grenfell Tower [47], The Marina Torch [48] and The China Tele­
com Building [49].
Ignition and Causes to Fatal Facade Fires
The Grenfell Tower and The Marina Torch fires shows that a combustible facade and a fire
in a compartment, can lead to a fatal facade fire. The China Telecom building fire shows
that a faulty air condition unit and a combustible facade leads to a devastating fire too. The
combination of combustible facade elements, and piloted ignition can potentially end up in a
large fire, that spreads rapidly.
China has over the past years experienced several fires in high­rise buildings, with a sig­
nificant number of casualties. According to an article from inside telecom, this could be
prevented or at least decreased, if the government implemented more strict laws regarding
fire safety [46].
Most facade fires start with a fire ignited inside, and then it spreads to the exterior. The
second most common way a facade fire starts are by external ignition, such as balcony fires,
fires started by a weed burner or an outdoor barbecue [41]. An external ignition can also be
caused by fireworks [44], or by rubbish being ignited by the bottom of a facade [6].
Research shows that when using the combustible ACM­ PE cladding system, the integrity of
the cavity is not ensured [43] and can cause a rapid fire spread in the facade [42]. Statistics
shows that most facade fires have occurred in countries, where there have been none or
poor regulation control on combustible materials used in the exterior wall [41].
Changes Caused by Past Facade Fires
The facade fires through the years have gained attention. In some cases, like the Grenfell
Tower, reasons to why and how the fire turned so fatal have been investigated. Historically
fire safety regulations have been formed by tragic disasters [50]. Following, but not limited
to, are regulations and recommendations that have been enforced or purposed due to the
last two decades of facade fires:
12
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:25==
• After the Grenfell Tower fire United Kingdom re­enforced stricter requirements in their
fire regulations, banning the use of combustible materials on the outside of tall buildings
[42].
• Due to facade fires in London, Honolulu, and Dubai the large property insurance com­
pany FM Global has suggested that there should be an increase in the use of sprin­
klers in tall buildings. With interior sprinkling activated, often only the exterior gets
damaged, and people can safely evacuate the building. Furthermore, FM Global has
recommended a prohibition of combustible facade materials [44].
• The new codes from IEBC (International Existing Building Code) edition 2021, states
that if an exterior wall covering or envelope is added or replaced to an existing external
wall, the new covering/envelope must follow the most recent requirements from IEBC
[51].
2.4.3
Facade Fires During the Thesis Period
In the short period of working with this thesis, three large facade fires occurred in high­rise
buildings. The China Telecom fire, which is described in section 2.4.2, occurred on the 16th
of September. Then a facade caught on fire in a 24­story tall building in Istanbul on the 15th
of October [52], and lastly a 35­story residential building in Dubai was damaged by a facade
fire on the 7th of November [53]. That three facades fires occur in the short period of thee
months, emphasize the importance of incorporating fire safety precautions when applying
facades of combustible materials.
2.5
Case: Stavnsholtskolen
The case about Stavnsholtskolen is part of the background information, even though it might
not seem to resonate with the high­rise buildings in densely built areas. However, it is rele­
vant, since it was mainly constructed in wood, with wooden facades, and fire “leapt” over a
rather large distance of air to another wooden facade.
In the morning on March the 27th, 1973 the school, Stavnsholtskolen, burned down. The
fairly new school on 12.000 m², had only a few hundred square meters left when the firemen
had contained and extinguished the fire [54]. Even though the school was fulfilling all the
requirements from the building regulations, and the fire department was at the scene no
later than 20 minutes after ignition of the fire, it ended up catastrophic in terms of property
damage. Luckily no casualties occurred. The firemen tried to “cut off” the fire, to prevent
spreading, but the fire was too strong, so it did not prevent fire propagation [55]. To find out
why it went so wrong, the fire was investigated thoroughly. Fire tests were performed on the
remaining part of the school and in a laboratory. The results from the tests, combined with
the fire department’s observations during the fire, and the property damage were analyzed
by a workgroup established by ATV – the Danish Academy of Technical Sciences (In Danish:
ATV ­ Akademiet for de Tekniske Videnskaber) [54].
To understand how the fire could turn out so severe, a description of how the school was
built is necessary. The school was a new “open plan” concept school. The structure was
constructed with a steel column/beam system. For room dividers, a light construction was
used. However, only some of the room dividers went all the way up to the ceiling. Some
dividers were only a little higher than a person, thus creating a semi open “school landscape”
[56]. The roof was constructed of load bearing wooden roof cassettes. To add to the open
plan concept, outdoor atriums with the size of 14.4 x 9.6 m were placed several places
throughout the school. The atriums’ functions were supposed to be fire sectioning and part
of the escape route. The atriums were used as a fire wall. The facades in the atriums were
also clad with wood, more specific glue laminated timber (glulam) [54].
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
13
==PAGE:26==
Figure 2.6: Aerial photo of the Stavnsholtskole, after the fire [57]
The conclusions from the workgroup, established by ATV, were that the most significant el­
ement for the duration and fierceness of the fire was the construction of the wooden roof
cassettes. These had a surface area two to three times larger than the floor area and con­
tributed to largely 80% of the fuel load. Furthermore, the large­scale tests performed in the
remaining part of the school showed that if there were a fire in one side of the atrium yard,
flame temperature could reach between 750­850°C. The thermal radiation, in the atrium,
would reach between 14­65 kW/m². With this radiation the dimensional critical radiation for
the glulam facades was exceeded, and the opposite facade (the non­burning one), would
receive high enough radiation to ignite, and break the windows too. Because of this the
fire would spread into a new fire section. With this discovery the atriums’ purposes as fire
sectioning and safe escape route, were not fulfilled [54].
At the time of the school’s construction the building regulations allowed atriums to be used as
fire sectioning, and escape routes. The fire proved that the dimensions of the atriums were
inadequate. As a consequence, authorities started questioning the atriums’ use as a fire wall
and to be part of the escape route. This made the Ministry of Housing add an appendix to
the building regulation’s fire prevention procedures regarding atrium yards and other narrow
passageways [58].
14
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:27==
3
Theory
3.1
Heat Transfer
There are five forms of heat transfer: Convention, conduction, radiation, advection, and
latent. Convention, conduction, and radiation are of relevance in this project and will be
described. The section regarding radiation will be more thorough than the others, since
this form of heat transfer is of great importance regarding fire spread to adjacent facades.
Advection and latent heat transfer will not be covered.
Often convention, conduction, and radiation are present in a fire, however one will be more
dominant, depending on the type fire and fire duration[23, Chapter 2].
3.1.1
Convection
Convection is exchange of heat between an object and its environment due to fluid motion
[59]. This can be between a gas and liquid, or gas and solid. Temperature changes drives the
convection, e.g. if the temperature rises the density of air decreases and moves upward. If
the temperature is cooled the density of the air gets denser, and descends. This is buoyancy
driven flows, which are referred to as either free or natural convection. If wind is present and
impacts the flow, then it is referred to as forced convection. Convective heat transfer is most
dominant in the beginning of the fire, when the radiation is low [23].
The equation to describe the convective heat transfer is:
˙q′′ = hc∆T
[W/m²]
(3.1)
Where hc is the convective heat transfer coefficient, which is not a material dependent con­
stant. The heat transfer coefficient is mainly dependent on flow­ and temperature parame­
ters. The range for hc for free convection is 5­25 W/m²·K, and for forced convection 10­500
W/m²·K in air. The convective heat transfer coefficient can be determined from equation 3.2
Nu = hl
k
(3.2)
Where k is the thermal conductivity, l is the characteristic length of the surface, and Nu is the
local Nusselt number. The Nusselt number is the ratio of temperature gradients. For natural
convection, by a vertical plate, the Nusselt number can be determined from equation 3.3 if
the flow is laminar (104 < Gr·Pr < 109) or equation 3.4 if the flow is turbulent (Gr·Pr > 109).
For laminar flow:
Nu = 0.59(Gr · Pr)
1
4
(3.3)
For turbulent flow:
Nu = 0.13(Gr · Pr)
1
3
(3.4)
To determine whether laminar or turbulent flow is present, the Grashof and Prandtl numbers
are used, see equation 3.5:
Gr = gl3(ρ∞−ρ)
ρν2
= gl3β∆T
ν2
and
Pr = µcp
k
(3.5)
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
15
==PAGE:28==
Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient in Regard to Adjacent Buildings
The convective heat transfer coefficient is not of great importance when there is no wind, in
regards to the heat influence on an adjacent building. When there is no wind, there is free
convection only moving vertical from the fire. Here the convection is in the smoke and fire
moving upward on the facade, at which the fire originated. If there is wind present (forced
convection), and it is blowing in the direction towards another building, the convective heat
transfer will occur in regards of smoke and flames moving towards the adjacent building.
Then the convective heat transfer will possibly have an influence on an adjacent building.
However, if there is enough wind, it could mix the smoke with cold air, and hence cool the
smoke, and decrease some of the convective heat transfer influence.
3.1.2
Conduction
Conduction as a heat transfer mode occurs as molecular energy transport [22]. It is when
heat is in direct contact with either a solid, which is most common, or a fluid and heat transfers
to said solid or fluid [23]. For steady state conduction Fourier’s law of heat conduction can
be applied, and is written in equation 3.6.
˙qx′′ = −kdT
dx
[W/m²]
(3.6)
Where:
˙qx′′ =
(dqx
dt
)
/A
[W/m²]
(3.7)
With A being the area in m², through which heat is being transferred into. k is the thermal
conductivity with unit W/m·K. The thermal conductivity is dependent on temperature, and
most values found in reference works is either for T=0°C or T=20°C. T is either in °C or K,
and x is in m [23].
Fire is a transient occurrence, where the thermal conductivity is not steady. For non­steady
state conductivity, the temperature will change over time, and can be expressed as [35]:
∂T
∂t = k
ρc
(∂2T
∂x2 + ∂2T
∂y2 + ∂2T
∂z2
)
(3.8)
Where k/ρc = α, and is the thermal diffusivity [23].
3.1.3
Radiation
Radiation is heat transfer by electromagnetic waves, which ranges from visible light to in­
frared on the electromagnetic spectrum. The wavelengths are ranging from λ=0.4 to 100
μm³. As prior mentioned convection is dominant in the beginning of the fire, where the tem­
perature is low (150­200°C). Radiation either becomes dominant at higher temperatures (>
400°C) or when the fuel bed increases beyond ∼0.3 m [23]. Radiation can be absorbed,
transmitted, or reflected at any surface of a body, and is described as α + ρ + τ = 1 and is
visualized in figure 3.1.
16
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:29==
Figure 3.1: Incoming radiation on a surface, redrawn from [59].
Stefan­Boltzmann’s equation describes the total emissive power:
E = εσT 4
[W/m²]
(3.9)
Where T is in K, σ is Stefan Boltzmann constant on 5.67 · 10−8 W/m2K4, ε is the emissivity,
which is described in section 3.1.3 ­ Emissivity.
The intensity of radiant energy reaching a surface is given in equation 3.10, which consider
the geometrical relationship between the emitter and receiver.
˙q′′ = ϕεσT 4
[W/m²]
(3.10)
ϕ is the geometrical relationship between the emitter and receiver, which is referred to as
either configuration factor or view factor. This is further elaborated in section 3.1.3 ­ Config­
uration Factor.
Emissivity
Emissivity is according to Drysdale “a measure of the efficiency of the surface as a radiator”
[23], and have a value between 0 ­ 1.
The ideal emitter is a black­body, where ε = 1, and the total amount of energy emitted per
unit area of an ideal emitter is:
Eb,λ =
2 · πc2hλ−5
exp(ch/λκT) −1
(3.11)
However real surfaces are not ideal black body emitters, and have an ε < 1, and can depend
on wavelength. This surface can be defined as:
ελ = Eλ
Eb,λ
(3.12)
Where Eλ is the emissive power of the real surface and can be found in reference works such
as Drysdale, figure 2.18(b)[23]. It is convenient to introduce ‘grey body’ which is an ‘ideal,
non­black’ body, where the ε is independent on the wavelength. This is an approximation;
yet, it allows the use of the Stefan Boltzmann equation (Total emissive power)[23].
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
17
==PAGE:30==
Kirchhoff’s law states that the emissivity equals the absorption coefficient for a medium with
the same temperature and wavelength (αλ(λ, T) = ελ(λ, T)), however for the grey­body
assumption (α(T) = ε(T)) [35].
Configuration Factor
In relation to radiation between buildings, the configuration factor is how an exposed building
sees the radiation from the burning building.
Stefan­Boltzmann equation is in equation 3.13 rewritten to include the finite­to­finite area
configuration factor’ and area of radiator.

3.2
Wood Properties and Fire Spread
3.2.1
Fire Dynamics Properties of Wood
When wood is exposed to heating at raised temperatures decomposition of wood occurs, this
process is called pyrolysis. When the pyrolysis process takes place two gaseous products
are created. One product avoids the forming of solid residue, and the other product turns into
char. When charring is formed the decomposition of wood under the char is decelerated [60].
The pyrolysis, and char formation of wood, varies between the different wooden species, and
18
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:31==
their thermal properties. Some important properties are mentioned below. The properties
are for pine wood, if there is no value for pine wood, the general value for wood is noted.
• Ignition temperature. Also called firepoint. The ignition temperature is the tempera­
ture needed before a fuel can be ignited. The temperature is measured on the solid
fuel’s surface. The ignition temperature depends on the amount of concentrated de­
composed fuel and how the fuel is heated [35]. Depending on the sources used, the
ignition temperature for pinewood varies between 349­446°C [61].
• Ignition time is the time it takes before piloted ignition takes place. With equation 3.16
it is possible to calculate it [19, chapter 36]. There are different equations to calculate
the ignition temperature, since it is dependent on whether the solid is thermally thick or
thermally thin.
tig(thick) =
π
4 kρcp(Tig −T0)2
( ˙qe′′ −χ ˙qcr′′)2
and
tig(thin) = ρcpd(Tig −T0)2
˙qe′′ −χ ˙qcr′′
(3.16)
Where k, ρ, c is the thermal conductivity, density and specific heat. Tig is the ignition
temperature, and T0 is the ambient temperature. ˙qe′′ is the incident heat flux and ˙qcr′′
is the critical heat flux. χ is the fraction of the approximated heat loss which occurred
from heating up the solid to ignition temperature. d is the thickness of the solid [19,
chapter 36].
• Critical heat flux. It is the lowest needed heat flux exposed onto an object to ignite
it. The critical heat flux can be calculated if the ambient and ignition temperature are
known, see equation 3.17.
˙qcr′′ = σ(T 4
ig −T 4
0 )
(3.17)
Literature have also given some values for the critical heat flux, according to SFPE
Handbook, chapter 86, the critical heat flux for spontaneous ignition on wood is 33.5
kW/m², and for piloted ignition is 12.5 kW/m² [19, chapter 86].
• Surface temperature of a material outside the reach of flames, and the minimum sur­
face temperature of the fuel, this is explained in section 3.2.2 ­ Fire Spread on Wood.
• Specific heat capacity. Which is the amount of heat (J) absorbed per unit mass (kg)
when the temperature of the fuel increases by one K. The specific heat for pinewood
is 2.3 kJ/(kg·K) [62].
• Density: The charring rate is dependent on the density. If the density is low the charring
rate is faster, than compared to more dense wood types [63]. The density for the pine
wood used in this project is calculated in appendix E, and ranges from 417 to 624 kg/m³.
• Thermal conduction. The thermal conduction is wood’s ability to conduct heat. Re­
ducing the thermal conduction because an increase in void volume, causes increased
flame spread rates [63]. The thermal conductivity for pinewood is 0.12 W/mK [62].
However, the thermal conduction is dependent on temperature. The prior given ther­
mal conductivity for wood, is when ambient temperature is 20°C.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
19
==PAGE:32==
3.2.2
Fire Spread on Wood
In this section upward flame spread in a solid fuel is described.
Vertical flame spread is faster than horizontal flame spread, due to the natural buoyancy
of flames. With natural buoyancy of flames’ hot gasses and upwards flame flow, a larger
pre­heated area is created, than compared to downward and lateral flame spread [23].
There are multiple conditions which influence the speed and spread of flames:
• Thickness of solid fuel. When the fuel is thin the heating of the material is faster, and
the flame spread increases. When the fuel is thicker, more energy is used to heat it
up, because there is more mass. This creates a smaller pre­heated area, and flames
propagate slower [23].
• Temperature of the fuel. If the fuel temperature increases the rate of flame spread
increases [23].
• Flat surface or edge. Flame spread occurs faster, when it is by an edge or corner
compared to a flat surface [23].
• Pressure. When the atmospheric pressure is increased, an increased flame spread
is observed. When pressure is increased, the effective heat transfer coefficient is in­
creased, since it is dependent on either Reynold’s number, for forced convection, or
the Grasfhof number, for natural convection [23].
• Wind flow. When wind flows in the direction of the flame spread, it is common to observe
the wind aiding the flame spread. However, if the degree of turbulence is increased,
the aiding influence of the wind flow is decreased significantly [23].
The equation for flame spread on a solid surface, such as wood is:
v =
˙q′′δf
ρcl(Tig −Ts)
[m/s]
(3.18)
Where ˙q′′ is the heat flux effect from the flames. It has been determined from prior exper­
iments, that the flame heat flux is 25 kW/m² for upward moving flame spread, from flames
ranging from 0.2 to 2 m in height. Additionally, for upward flame spread, the flame heat flux
is independent on the fuel. δf is the distance, in m, on the fuel, which is exposed to direct
contact to the flames. ρ is the density of the fuel [kg/m³]. c is the specific heat capacity of
the fuel [kJ/(kg·K)]. l is the thickness of the fuel, in m. Tig is the ignition temperature of the
fuel, which is either in °C or K, however it is important that Tig and Ts has the same unit. Ts
is the temperature of the surface of the fuel outside the area heated by flames. The value of
Ts has to by higher than the fuel’s Ts,min to have continuous flaming. Ts,min is the minimum
temperature the surface of the fuel has to be, before either ignition or flame propagation can
occur [35].
3.3
How a Fire Starts in a Facade
Fire in high­rise facades can occur in three basic ways, which are described in this section.
3.3.1
From One Building to Another Building
Fire can spread from one burning building to a non­burning building. If the burning building is
close to the non­burning building, the fire can spread with direct flame contact, heat radiating
from the fire and/or flying brands and embers. If flying brands land on an already heated
surface, the potential for ignition is increased. If there is more distance between the burning
20
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:33==
building and non­burning building, fire can spread to the non­burning building. For this case
of fire spread, the non­burning building needs to be exposed for strong enough radiation for
enough time to quell the integrity of the wall or barrier. According to NFPA 80A and BR 187
(BR ­ Building Research Establishment) a fire spread caused by radiation can occur when
there is direct line­of­sight between radiating building and non­burning building. If a radiating
building has a non­combustible exterior barrier, it is only the flame front, that is considered
the radiator. If the exterior barrier is constructed in a combustible material, the whole front
can act as a radiator [19, Chapter 86].
3.3.2
From a Window/Compartment in The Building
Flames from a compartment can extend to the facade trough a window, either if was open
or burst during the fire due to high temperatures in the compartment. Flames from a window
can extend more than 5 m above the top of the window. Additionally hot gases from the
window at 1.75 m above to top of the window, can reach temperatures between 400­600 °C,
which is a high enough temperature to ignite untreated wood. A facade can be ignited in the
cavity, if the exterior wall structure is ignited from within a compartment, and the integrity fails,
or there are an opening into the cavity. An oping into a cavity, if not due to loss of integrity,
can be caused by a fire stop not implemented correctly [19, Chapter 86].
Fire in the compartments behind the facade fire, contributes to the collective heat radiation
towards the adjacent building.
3.3.3
Fire Near the Facade
A facade can be ignited by fires occurring close to the facade. It has previously been ob­
served that the following can start a facade fire [6]:
• Rubbish gathered at ground level, if ignited near the bottom of a facade.
• Electrical equipment, which can be placed at ground level near facade, directly on the
facade or on roof, can comprise electrical short circuits.
• A barbeque accident either by ground level or at a balcony, can ignite a facade.
• A weed burner getting to close to the bottom of a facade.
• Fireworks set off close to the building, and when it explodes, it hits and ignites the
facade.
3.4
CFD
CFD is a numerical tool used to model and simulate physical models including fluid flows.
The Navier­Stokes equations describe any kind of fluid, with three governing equations: Con­
servation of mass, momentum, and energy. There are no analytical solutions to the Navier­
Stokes equations, they can only be solved numerically. FDS numerically solves a reduced
form of the Navier­Stokes equations, which is appropriate for low speed, thermally driven
flow with a focus on smoke and heat transport from fire.
3.4.1
FDS - Fire Dynamics Simulator
FDS is a CFD software, developed to simulate thermally driven flows in buildings, and can be
used to simulate fire related problems. Furthermore it can effectively be utilized for low­speed
transport of heat and combustion, and radiative and convective heat transfer.
FDS is created in a collaboration between VTT (Technical Research Centre of Finland) and
NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology), and the current version available and
used in this project is FDS 6.7.9.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
21
==PAGE:34==
3.4.2
Visualization Tools
Smokeview (SMW) is a visualization program. A tool to use when visualizing numerical pre­
dictions produced by FDS and CFAST. Smokeview can be used as part of the post process­
ing of data from the fire models. Smokeview can visualize and generate data as 2D contour
slices and vector plots as are relevant for this project. Furthermore, it is able to generate 3D
contours, however not relevant here. Smokeview is in this project used for visualization of
the small FDS­simulation described in section 4.2.
PyroSim is a software produced by Thunderhead Engineering. PyroSim can be used as
a graphical user interface for FDS, and as a visualization tool to view and analyze data
from FDS simulations. PyroSim is able to visualize the same slice files and vector plots
as Smokeview but is running more efficiently than Smokeview when processing large FDS
simulations.
3.4.3
Mode of Operation
FDS’ default mode of operation is Very Large Eddy Simulations (VLES). VLES is a simplified
version of the Navier­Stokes equations, a spatial filter is applied to the equations. For very
fine grid resolution it is possible to use Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS). However this is
computational very heavy, and a supercomputer is needed for running such simulations. For
this type of project, VLES is adequate to use.
3.4.4
Combustion
The “simply chemistry” combustion model is used for gas phase combustion, where a reac­
tion of fuel vapor and oxygen takes place. The reaction is only controlled by mixing, where
it is assumed that fuel and oxygen react at an infinitely fast pace. The model uses fuels with
simple chemistry, primarily containing the following molecules: C, H, O and N. When using
this model, it is assumed that the chemical reaction follows equation 3.19.
CxHyOzNv + νO2O2 →νCO2CO2 + νH2OH2O + νCOCO + νSSoot + νN2N2
(3.19)
The reaction is done in a single step, and produces a product containing a mixture of H2O,
CO2, soot, and carbon monoxide, based on the reactant and fuel source defined [64].
3.4.5
Pyrolysis
Pyrolysis is a solid phase combustion, that is when a material undergoes a phase change
from a solid to a gas phase. During pyrolysis a generation of fuel vapor takes place at a
solid surface. In FDS pyrolysis depends largely on the material properties, such as, but not
restricted to, conductivity, density and specific heat. The material properties indicate how
fast the material heats up and how the material burns during a fire. The pyrolysis model
requires that a gas phase reaction has been defined. It is only possible to use one pyrolysis
model at a time. In this project, at simple pyrolysis model for flame spread in a solid material
have been used.
Flame Spread
Flame spread in FDS is activated by prescribing an ignition temperature. Ignition temperature
is a surface parameter and not a material parameter. When a surface reaches the prescribed
ignition temperature, the material burns at a user prescribed heat release rate. However, in
FDS there is no coupling between burning rate and surface temperature, implying that heat
transfer in the solid is calculated independently on the surface temperature. This might lead
to a too large increase in surface temperature.
3.4.6
Extinction
The mixing­controlled reaction model for combustion assumes that fuel and oxygen always
react infinitely fast. It does not consider the heat conditions and reactant concentration,
22
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:35==
hence if there is heat and reactant enough for combustion to take place. Therefore an ex­
tinction model is needed to tell FDS, when to stop the combustion processes.
FDS has two extinction models. Extinction model l, which considers oxygen available and
cell temperature at the beginning of each time step. It uses equation 3.20 to find the limiting
oxygen concentration, Xo2,ijk. The limiting oxygen concentration is dependent on the cell bulk
temperature, Tijk. Local combustion is suppressed when Xo2,ijk < Xo2,lim.
XO2,lim(Tijk) =
{
XOI
( TOI−Tijk
TOI−T∞
)
Tijk < Tfb
0
Tijk ≥Tfb
(3.20)
Furthermore, when Extinction model 1 is used, local suppression of combustion will not occur
when the cell temperature is 600°C or above even if there is no oxygen available. The 600°C
indicate that flashover have occurred, and experiments have shown that the upper layer
oxygen concentration drops to zero during flashover [65]. Extinction model 1 is intended
for simulations with coarse grids, where details of flaming and flame temperature cannot be
resolved.
Extinction 2 is the default extinction model in FDS and is also used here. Extinction 2 takes
fuel and its thermophysical properties of the reactants and products, oxygen, and tempera­
ture in the cell into consideration at the beginning of each timestep. If the heat release rate
potential cannot raise the temperature in the cell to the critical flame temperature, TCFT, or
higher, local combustion is suppressed [65].
3.4.7
Heat Transfer and Thermal Properties
How the three modes of heat transfer and thermal properties for mainly solids are modelled
in FDS, are described in the sections below.
Conductive Heat Transfer
In FDS conduction is assumed to move in one direction, which is normal to the surface. Heat
conduction in a solid, is calculated in FDS with equation 3.21.
ρscs
∂Ts
∂t = ∂

∂x

Where qs’’’, the source term, is given in equation 3.22, and consists of two parts. qs,c’’’ covers
the chemical reactions, that is the heat production loss rate, from one of the chosen pyrolysis
models for solids and liquids. qs,r’’’ is the radiative absorption, which also includes emission.
˙q′′′

(3.22)
Here 1D heat transfer is used, and adequate since it transspires in homogeneous materials.
However, issues in corners with transient layers from cold to warm gas can occur [65].

The equation for convective heat transfer is given in equation 3.23 and 3.24.

Since the FDS solver utilized for this project is VLES, the convective heat transfer coefficient
h, is calculated on the basis of a combination of natural and forced convection correlations.
Here Tg is the gas temperature and Tw is the temperature near the surface. Furthermore, C
is an empirical coefficient for natural convection, here applied for a vertical plate so C = 1.31,
L is a characteristic length, k is the thermal conductivity of the gas, and δn is the gas phase
cell size [65].
Radiative Heat Transfer
The thermal radiation contribution in the energy equation is given in equation 3.25 and 3.26.
Solving for the radiation contribution can be a rather demanding process and is hence solved
with the Finite Volume Method (FVM).
˙q′′′

Thermal Properties
For the solid materials near the fire, the thermal properties are of great importance. The
thermal properties that can be specified for a material are listed in table 3.1

By default, in FDS, the emissivity is set to 0.9, if nothing else is specified by the user. Con­

function RAMP can be activated [64].
Per default, if a material is one cell thick or smaller, the innermost layer of the material is
exposed to the air temperature on the back side. It is possible to specify the back side
boundary conditions. If the back of the wall or material is exposed to ambient conditions, the
attribute BACKING=’VOID’ is appropriate to use [64].

Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
