4
Method
4.1
Experimental Work
In the project several experiments have been conducted. Four experiments, where a mock­
up of a wooden facade was ignited. Three out of the four experiments were influenced
by a PPV. Furthermore, bomb calorimeter measurements of the wooden products used in
the facade have been conducted. This chapter aims to describe the methods used in the
experiments and measurements.
The facade burning experiments, have been conducted in collaboration with Joakim Wåhlén,
a student on the DTU Master in fire safety. His focus was on the wind’s impact on the fire
spread, whereas the writer’s focus was on the temperature and heat flux caused by the
wooden facade fire.
4.1.1
Facade Burning
Experimental Set-Up
The facade is constructed with a wooden frame type structure. The wooden frame is covered
by two layers of fire safe gypsum boards. Outside on the gypsum board horizontal and
vertical wooden battens are mounted with screws. The facade cladding is installed on the
battens, this creates a ventilated void between the back wall and the facade cladding. The
facade cladding is mounted in the vertical tongue and groove method. Moelven Thermowood
Profile 581 was used as the facade cladding and had a moisture content between 3.2­4.5 %
see appendix B. For measurements and further detailing, see figure 4.1, 4.2 and appendix A
for drawings of the wooden facade. The whole construction is placed on three wooden laths
with wheels underneath. This makes it possible the easily move the whole wall construction
for safety reasons.
Figure 4.1: Front facing picture of the wall construction
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
25
==PAGE:38==
Four experiments were conducted with the facade:
Experiment, test 1: The wooden facade is exposed to a fire source.
Experiment, test 2: The wooden facade is exposed to a fire source, and variating wind ve­
locities.
Experiment, test 3: The wooden facade is exposed to a fire source and wind with a velocity
on 3.5 m/s and 5 m/s.
Experiment, test 4: The wooden facade is exposed to a fire source three times greater in
size, than the three other experiments, and exposed to a wind with a velocity on 2 m/s.
Figure 4.2: Drawing of the wall construction. TL: Side view. TR: Front view.
Fire Source
The test standard DS/ISO 13785, used for testing the performance of facades and facade
cladding, uses a propane gas burner, with a heat output on 100 kW (± 5 kW) as an ignition
source [66]. This corresponds with the same heat output as a wastebasket [22], and is not an
unrealistic fire size. This size of fire can also occur on a balcony with a BBQ, some rubbish
by the bottom of the building or an even larger heat output can occur from a window from
a compartment, where flashover has occurred. However, the fire source for the first three
experiments was a pan, Ø24 cm, filled with 408 g of methanol, having a heat output on 9.5
kW and an estimated burning time on 600 seconds. For experiment 4, three pans were
used, Ø20, Ø24, and Ø28 cm. Each pan contained 208 g of methanol. Here the source was
estimated to burn for relatively 11, 7, and 5 minutes depending on the size of the pan. See
calculations for the ignition source in appendix C. The heat output had to be scaled down
compared to the 100 kW, due to safety measures. The flame height had to be less than half
the height of the facade (> 0.90 m). It was determined that a calculated mean flame height
on 0.33 m, would be sufficient to ignite the facade cladding and still be safe. With 408 g of
methanol in a pan with a diameter on 24 cm, the fuel is according to calculations in appendix
C, able to burn for 600 seconds.
26
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:39==
Wind Source
The wind source was borrowed from Beredskab Øst, a positive pressure ventilator (PPV)
with variating wind speed. It was an older model, with power cord connected to its battery,
probably by the manufacturer Ramfan. The wind speed was controlled by up and down
buttons and showed in levels. Therefore, an anemometer was used to measure the wind
speed for each level before the fire tests. The wind speed used under the experiments were
between 2 – 5 m/s. The PPV was placed 2 m from the fire source, with a 45­degree angle
onto the facade.
Measurements
25 thermocouples in experiment test 2­4 and 33 thermocouples in experiment test 1 were
used to measure the temperature development during the experiments. Thermocouples of
type K with cord IEC­KX­2 from www.tc.co.uk were used. Thermocouples type K, usually
have an accuracy on +/­2°C or +/­0.75%, depending on which is greater [67]. The data was
collected with the data­logger Agilent 34970A, which had two 34901A: 20­Channel Armature
Multiplexer inserted. Agilent 34970A was connected to a PC, with the software BenchLink
data logger 3, which was used to collect and save the data from the thermocouples.
In the void(L1) between the gypsum boards and facade cladding, 12 thermocouples were
placed. In the same position, but directly on the facade(L2) further 12 thermocouples were
placed. For experiment test 1, further two by four thermocouples were placed respectively
60 cm(L3) and 120 cm(L4) in front of the facade. Figure 4.3 and 4.4 displays the setup of
the thermocouples on and in front the facade. See appendix D for thorough drawings of the
placement of the thermocouples.
Figure 4.3: Placement of thermocouples in the void(L1) behind the facade cladding
A device invented by Lars Schiøtt Sørensen was used to measure the heat flux one meter
away from the burning facade. Figure 4.4 displays the device, and its placement during the
experiment. A FLIR MSX (Multi­Spectral Dynamic Imaging) thermal camera, of the model
TG165, was used to measure temperature on the front of the facade during test 1 and take
thermal pictures of the front.
Each experiment was recorded by two cameras. One camera was placed in front of the
facade, and the second camera was placed to the side of the wall construction, facing the
void. Each camera was connected to a laptop with picture and video editing software.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
27
==PAGE:40==
Figure 4.4: TL: Heat flux measuring device in front of the facade. TR: Placement of thermocouples
in front of facade.
Safety Precautions
The experiments took place in DTU’s FireLab, building 122. The wall construction, was dur­
ing the experiments, placed under the large hood in the laboratory. The hood and ventilation
system are restricted in their usage, therefore it was during the experiments important to
have safety measures in place. For the experiments the following safety precautions were
taken:
• Flames may not touch the roof of the large hood continuously.
• The exhaust fan in the hood is turned all the way up to its maximum. If smoke spills out
from under the hood, and spread to the ambient room, the fire needs to be extinguished.
To extinguish the fire, the following equipment were available at all times:
• A water­filled hose, connected to a faucet and ready to turn on.
• Two pressure sprayers, filled with 16 liters of water in each.
• A powder extinguisher placed close to the experiment.
When building and setting up the experiment, the following were done to increase the safety
during the experiments:
• The wall construction was placed on three wooden laths with wheels underneath. Mak­
ing it possible to wheel out the construction in case the fire being too extensive.
• Wooden laths were placed with an incline between the wall and the laths with wheels,
supporting the wall. In case the wall construction needed to be wheeled out, the wall
had support, and would not fall over.
• A rope was tethered to the wall construction, so it could be pulled in case the wall
construction needed to be moved. The rope made sure, that during movement of the
wall, one would be in a safe distance from the fire.
• The cords of the thermocouples were gathered and rolled up, so they would not inter­
fere with the wheels, and obstruct the movement of the wall construction.
28
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:41==
• A wooden plate was mounted horizontal behind the wall, where the two Armature Mul­
tiplexer connected to all the thermocouples, could be placed during movement of the
wall construction.
Process of Experiments
Experiment, test 1
A pan, with a diameter on 24 cm was filled with 408 g of methanol and ignited. The pan was
placed under the wooden facade, so the fire from the source attacked the facade cladding
on the front and back side. During the experiment 33 thermocouples were used to measure
the temperature development. The heat flux measuring device was placed in front of the
fire, and not moved during the experiment1. Every 60th second the heat flux was noted. 4
minutes and 31 seconds after ignition the ignition source was removed from the facade. The
flames were continuously in contact with the hood.
Experiment, test 2
The same fire source and placement as in experiment, test 1 were used. During the experi­
ment 25 thermocouples were used to measure the temperature development. The device to
measure the heat flux, was used every 60th second, and removed from facing the fire after
taking the measurement. The wind machine was turned on after ignition of the fire source.
During the experiment the velocity of the wind was variating. After 7 minutes and 42 seconds
the fuel was depleted.
Experiment, test 3
The same fire source and placement as in experiment, test 1 were used. During the experi­
ment 25 thermocouples were used to measure the temperature development. The device to
measure the heat flux, was used every 60th second, and removed from facing the fire after
taking the measurement. The wind machine was turned on to 3.5 m/s 5 minutes and 40
seconds after ignition of the ignition source. 6 minutes and 48 seconds after ignition of the
ignition source the wind was increased to 5 m/s. The fuel depleted and the fire on the facade
and in the void died down 8 minutes and 16 seconds after ignition. The wind was shut down
10 minutes and 31 seconds after ignition.
Experiment, test 4
Three pans with a diameter on respectively 20 cm, 24 cm and 28 cm were each filled with 208
g of methanol and ignited. During the experiment 25 thermocouples were used to measure
the temperature development. The device to measure the heat flux, was used every 60th
second, and removed from facing the fire after taking the measurement. The wind machine
was turned on to 2 m/s 2 minutes and 31 seconds after ignition of fire source. 3 minutes and
43 seconds after ignition the Ø28 pan had burned all its fuel. 4 minutes and 29 seconds after
ignition the Ø24 pan had run out of fuel. 5 seconds after that the wind was shut down, due to
an extensive fire in the void, and flames were continuously touching the hood. At 5 minutes
and 20 seconds after ignition the small pan was removed from the facade. 19 seconds later
the pressure sprayers were used to extinguish the facade fire.
After experiment 1­3 the facade was rebuilt. Only the wooden battens and facade cladding
were rebuilt, since the rest of the construction could be reused. Placement of the thermo­
couples were kept the same.
1This gave faulty results. The device is only supposed to be facing the fire for 2­3 seconds, while measuring
the heat flux, and then removed.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
29
==PAGE:42==
4.1.2
Bomb Calorimeter
With a bomb calorimeter the heat of combustion can be determined. The combustion process
of the test species takes place in ideal conditions, and the test species undergo a complete
combustion. In this project three test species are used for testing. All three species are found
in the construction of the facade experiment. The markings on figure 4.5 shows where the
different wooden species are used in the construction.
Figure 4.5: Test species in the facade construction
The facade cladding used was Moelven Thermowood Profile 581, which was planed thermo­
treated pinewood. The wooden battens were of pressure impregnated pinewood. The frame
was of pinewood constructual timber.
The test species were prepared before the bomb calorimeter measurements could take
place. Each of the test species was original a piece of wood. To test the wood in the bomb
calorimeter, the wood to be in a saw­dust­state. The sawdust state of each test species are
seen in figure 4.6.
Figure 4.6: Test species, in the following order: Wooden battens, frame and facade cladding

Process of Bomb Calorimeter Experiment
Before tests with test species:
First the bomb calorimeter needed to be calibrated. This was done with pelletized IKA C
723 Benzoic Acid. The calibration value used further in the experiments was 8464 J/K. The
calibration value was based on the average over multiple Benzoic Acid measurements with
the bomb calorimeter.
Test with test species:
A standard decomposition vessel was prepared with a test specie; a crucible was placed on
a scale, then the tare button was used, so only the weight of the test specie was measured.
The weight of each test specie is found in table 4.1. A cotton thread was with tweezers,
coiled around the ignition wire, and the crucible placed in the crucible holder. The tweezers
was used to make sure that the cotton tread was immersed in the test specie. The cover with
the test specie was placed on the lower section of the decomposition vessel and the union
nut screwed on tight by hand.
Figure 4.7: 1­3: Decomposition vessel C 5010. 1: Cover with electric ignition contact, ignition wire
and crucible holder. 2: Lower section. 3: Union nut. 4: Venting button 5: Crucible. 6: Handle. 7:
Tweezers. 8: Ignition adapter. 9: Cotton thread.
Using the oxygen station IKA C 248, the decomposition vessel was filled with oxygen to
approximately 30 bar. On top of the cover, the ignition adaptor was slid on. Then the de­
composition vessel was ready for testing. The decomposition vessel was placed into the
inner vessel of the bomb calorimeter between the three locating bolts.
The bomb calorimeter was prepared for testing. In the display menu ”Prepare” was chosen,
the following was entered in the “Prepare a test” menu:
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
31
==PAGE:44==

Calibration is set to 0, since this means that the bomb calorimeter is running a test, and not
a calibration. There is only one decomposition vessel in use. The energy generated by the
cotton thread is 50 J, which is specified in menu item “QExt1”. There is not used additional
burning aids, hence “QExt2” is set to 0.
2 liters of water was poured into the tank filler. Here the temperature had to be between 19°C
to 24°C. The temperature of the water was measured with a type K thermocouple connected
to the data­logger Agilent 34970A.
Figure 4.8: 1: IKA Calorimeter System C 200. 2: Tank filler. 3 Menu display. 4 Measuring cell cover
5: C 248 – Oxygen filling station
When the measuring cell cover was closed, the test could begin. The first action of the bomb
calorimeter test was filling the water storage and equalizing, which took 3 minutes. After­
ward the main test began. Both for equalizing and main test the temperature development
was written down every 12th second. After 8.2 minutes the testing was done, and a heat
of combustion value was showed on the display. The results can be observed in section
6.3. The test was done nine times. Three times each for each wooden species used in the
construction of the facade.

Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings

4.2
FDS
This section describes the set­up of the FDS model, which simulates experiment, test 1 of
the facade burning experiments. The full code is in appendix K.
4.2.1
Time
The FDS code is set to simulate for 950 seconds. This equals ∼15 minutes, which was the
time the experimental, test 1 lasted before it was terminated.
4.2.2
Meshes and Cell Sizes
The model has meshes in variating cell sizes, depending on the placement of the mesh.
The meshes close to the fire, the facade, and where smoke and wind speed are present the
mesh is finer. This is done to ensure better resolution and better calculations of the flame
spread and temperature development. In the fire and smoke region the cell size will be kept
constant. In places where there lower velocity and low smoke spread the mesh is coarser. To
determine the mesh resolution the non­dimensional expression D*/δx is used. Where δx is
the nominal size of a mesh cell² and D* is the characteristic fire diameter. The characteristic
fire diameter is found with equation 4.1.
D∗=
(
˙Q
ρ∞cpT∞√g
) 2
5
(4.1)
Table 4.3 lists the values used to calculate the characteristic fire diameter, and the minimum
and maximum cell size appropriate near the ignition source. D*/δx should be in the range of
4­16 [68].

Based on the cell size range found in table 4.3, the cell size used for the fine meshes is
0.02x0.02x0.02 m. For the coarser mesh, where a fine resolution is not necessary the cell
size is determined to be 0.10x0.10x0.10 m.
4.2.3
Reaction and Fire
During the experimental tests, the ignition source was methanol. Methanol in the FDS model
is defined on the REAC line, and the properties used for methanol are presented in table 4.4.
Table 4.4: Properties for methanol.

[19]
The fire was modelled to have a maximum heat release rate (HRR) on 14 kW. This was
modelled on the SURF line, where the HRRPUA was set to be 324.1, and corresponding
ignition source top surface on 0.043m². In the experimental test, an ignition source on 9.5
kW was used. The 14 kW in the FDS model is larger, due to test simulations, that showed
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
33
==PAGE:46==
temperature curves and heat fluxes in the FDS model is more comparable to the experimental
test, with an increased HRR. It was noted from the test results from the experimental, test
1, that there was a time period before the fire from the ignitions source was burning at its
maximum capacity. Therefore the function RAMP_Q is applied to the code. The fire from
the ignition source is modelled to be at its maximum heat release 86 seconds after ignition.
Additionally the RAMP function is used to simulate the removal of the fire source, which is
removed after 271 seconds. This is a model, where radiation from flames is an important
part of the heat transfer, therefore the number of radiation angles in the FDS model is set to
500.
4.2.4
Materials and Surfaces
Three materials are present in the model: Wood, gypsum, and steel. There are three different
wooden materials in the model, and these cover the wooden frame, wooden battens, and
the facade cladding. The properties applied to each of the wooden materials and wooden
surfaces are listed in table 4.5. Ignition temperature and HRRPUA are modelled on the
SURF line, since these are surface properties and not material properties. The BURN_AWAY
function is applied for the surfaces for facade cladding and wooden battens. This function is
added, so an obstacle with the surface parameter, is decomposed after it has been pyrolyzed.
In the visualization of the simulation, part of the material, which is burned away, is removed.

4.2.5
Surfaces of Exterior Mesh Boundaries
The exterior boundaries are defined as VENT and placed on the exterior side of meshes
where one or more sides are not aligned to other meshes. The VENT is given the SURF_ID
= ‘OPEN’. This is done because only a small part of the FireLab is included in the model.
FireLab has a large volume, and can contribute with enough makeup air, so the fire does not
suffer from oxygen starvation, when the exhaust is turned on.
4.2.6
Mechanical Ventilation
The exhaust fan in FireLab is of the brand EXHAUSTO, type: BESF28043EC. According to
the exhaust fan’s data sheet, the maximum capacity is 1870 m³/h. Given that there is a little
length in the piping, and the piping have two elbows, there must be some pressure loss in
the exhaust system. It is estimated, that when the vent is turned to maximum exhaust, the
amount of air exhausted is 1600 m³/h. To model this into FDS, the airflow is needed in m/s.
This can be calculated as shown in equation 4.2.
Qair = v · (π · r2) →v = Qair
π · r2
(4.2)
The vent has been measured to have a diameter on 52 cm. This gives the following airflow:
v = Qair
π · r2 =
1600 m3
h
π · (52cm
2
)2 = 2.09m
s
(4.3)
For the exhaust fan in FDS, a SURF line is written with VEL = 2.09. When the number for VEL
is positive, air will be removed from the model. Had the number been negative, air would be
added to the model. The SURF entry is called upon a VENT line, with a surface area close
to π · (52cm/2)2 = 0.21m2 To fit it to the grid, the dimension of the vent is 0.4 · 0.5 = 0.2m2.
4.2.7
Geometry
The FDS model is 3x3.8x3.6m and is only a piece of the FireLab. In the model the large
steel hood, the steel floor underneath the hood, and the wall construction with the facade
are modelled with obstructions (OBST). In figure 4.9 is the picture to the left displaying the
part of FireLab, that is of relevance in the FDS model, which is displayed to the right.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
35
==PAGE:48==
Figure 4.9: TL: Picture of the experimental setup in FireLab. TR: The geometry of the test setup in
the FDS model, visualized in Smokeview.
The wall construction is made up of a gypsum backing, which is 4 cm thick in FDS, equivalent
to the size of two cells. The framing, that was on the back of the gypsum plates in the
experiment, have not been modelled. The framing was considered not to have influence on
the fire. The wooden battens were modelled to be 2 cm thick in the horizontal direction, and
4 cm thick in the vertical direction. This is a little smaller than in the experiment, but this
were the measurements, that was closest the to real version and still fit into the grid. The
mesh is fine with 0.02³ sized cells near the fire, further away it gets coarser, which is why in
figure 4.9 the wooden battens change in size close to the edge of the wall construction. The
facade cladding is modelled to be one cell size thick; this is 1 mm thinner than the Moelven
Thermowood boards used.
The steel hood is made of four inclining sides. It is not possible in FDS to model a smooth
incline. The incline has been model with smaller rectangular pieces, which is shown in figure
4.10. It was important to have the sides to resemble an incline, since it would influence the
wind movement, speed, and air temperature above the wall construction.
Figure 4.10: The rectangular construction of the steel hood, seen from below. Visualized in Smoke­
view.
36
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:49==
4.2.8
Measuring Points
To be able to analyze and compare simulation results with the results from the experiment,
measuring points are added to the model. Slice files (SLCF) to visualize temperature, are
added so it is possible to observe the temperature development by each of the thermocou­
ples. Furthermore, slice files have been added to go through the center of the fire, in both
the X and Y direction.
Slice files to display the velocity have been added in the center of the exhaust, in front of the
exhaust and in the center of the fire. Here VECTOR=.TRUE. is activated, making it possible
to visualize the movement of air with vectors.
Boundary files (BNDF) are applied for ‘NET HEAT FLUX’ and ‘WALL TEMPERATURE’, mak­
ing it possible to visualize respectively the heat flux on the HT­meter and the wall temperature
on the facade cladding.
Point devices (DEVC) with QUANTITY= ’THERMOCOUPLE’ are placed in all the points
where there in the real experiment was placed a thermocouple. The output from the thermo­
couples, is by FDS given in a .csv file.
One surface device is placed by the HT­meter to measure the net heat flux, the output is
given in the same .csv file as the thermocouples.
4.2.9
HPC servers
Several simulations have been run to tune the model, and correct mistakes to have it cor­
respond to the experiment done in FireLab. The simulations have been running on DTU’s
HPC clusters. The access to the clusters has been through G­databar and the software
ThinLinc client. To start a simulation one must run a script in the lsf10sh terminal. The script
includes information on the placement of the code for the FDS model, number of cores to
use, it specifies how many cores to be assigned per MPI process, and how many machines
the computation can be spread out on.
The FDS model had 26 meshes divided out on 12 MPI processes. The meshes that would be
computational heavy, such as the finer meshes near the fire, had its own MPI process. This
was done to increase the efficiency and reduce the computational cost and time. 4 cores
were assigned to each MPI process.
4.3
Up-scaling of Experiment in FDS
The smaller FDS model of the facade test 1, has been compared to the results in section
6. Since the results are comparable, the model has been scaled up. Some things are the
same, such as surface and material parameters and REAC line. The size of the whole
domain, meshes, obstructions and fire are changed to simulate two adjacent facades, which
is placed outside in Danish climate.
4.3.1
Geometry
Six cases of different size domains are going to be run in FDS for the up scaling. The
model consists of two facades adjacent to one another. The facades are being constructed
in the same way, as in the small FDS model regarding material and surface parameters, and
thickness of the gypsum, wooden battens, and facade cladding. However, the height of the
facades is 17 m, and the width is 15 m. Ideally the model would be geometrically larger
and resemble Mjøstårnet which is 17m x 37m and 85.4 m high. The model is smaller due
to the computational power available. There is not any compartments behind the facades.
The distance between the two facades is 5 m, 7.5 m, and 10 m depending on the case.
The cases run for this part are listed in table 4.7.The distances are according to the pre­
accepted solutions. 10 m corresponds to two buildings each with open facades or glass
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
37
==PAGE:50==
facades, and each placed 5 meters from the shared property line. 7.5 m corresponds to one
building with an open or glass facade placed 5 m from shared property line, and the building
on the other side with a facade in material class B­s1,d0 placed 2.5 m from property line.
5 m corresponds to two buildings each with B­s1,d0 facades, and each placed 2.5 meters
from the shared property line. The facades are still modelled in wood, and hence not abiding
by the prescriptive­based codes. These simulations are done to see if it is possible to have
wooden buildings close to each other without decreasing the fire safety.
Figure 4.11: The geometry of the model, where the distance between facades is 10 m. Visualized in
Smokeview.
4.3.2
Ignition Sources
Two different fire scenarios are used, one fire scenario uses an ignition source placed by
the foot of the facade on the high­rise building. This is to simulate rubbish burning. The
ignition source used has a HRR on 100 kW, which is the energy release used in the ISO
test standard 13785­1 for intermediate­scale facade testing [66]. This fire size equals the
energy release from a waste basket, according to Enclosure Fire Dynamics [22]. This is not
an unrealistic ignition source. The area of the ignition source is 0.3m x 0.6m=0.18m², given
this the HRRPUA is 555.55 kW/m². The ignition source is placed, so it has access to the
ventilated cavity. The ignition source is removed after 20 minutes. The other fire scenario
use an ignition source placed 4.1 m up on the facade, and mimics flames being emitted from
a post­flashover compartment, where two windows have burst. In former experiments [19,
Chapter 25], heat fluxes up till 200 kW/m² have been measured. Hence for this fire scenario
two fires (each from the windows burst), are modelled with a HRRPUA = 200 kW/m². The
ignition source is removed after 20 minutes. According to Drysdale, the ignition source burns
out after 20 minutes [23, chapter 10]. The fire scenarios are listed in table 4.7
38
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings

4.3.3
Meshing
The method to determine the resolution of the mesh near the ignition source, is the same
as with the small FDS model. In table 4.8 the minimum and maximum cell sizes are listed
for both fire scenarios. For both fire scenarios the grid cell size by the ignition source is
0.05x0.05x0.05m. Where the evolution of the fire from the ignition source fire and flame
spread are estimated to occur the cell size is 0.1x0.1x0.1 m. Further away from the fire on
the facade, where there might not be much flame action the cell size is 0.2x0.2x0.2 m. The
mesh further away from the fire on the facade is coarser due to the large size of the model,
and not enough available time to solve a finer mesh in the whole model. The models for cases
with a 5 m distance between facades has 30 meshes divided out on 18 MPI processes. The
models with 7.5 m and 10 m distance have 33 meshes divided out on 18 MPI processes.

4.3.4
Weather Conditions
As with the small model, the sides of the model are open vents. There will then be enough
makeup air to avoid the fire being repressed from oxygen starvation. No wind is modelled,
as it is assumed that there is no wind influencing the ignition source and its fire spread, as
there where no wind in the experiment. The ambient start temperature is the yearly mean
temperature in Denmark, which is 9.1°C [72].
4.3.5
Time to Run the Simulation
The simulation is covering 60 minutes (3600 seconds), this time is chosen because BR18
§117 states that external walls should be constructed to prevent fire spread between different
fire­related units in external walls, in the time it takes to perform evacuation and the rescue
efforts of the emergency services, which is usually 60 minutes [9].
4.3.6
Measuring Points and Slice Files of Interest
Slice files (SLCF) in the middle of the ignition source in the X and Y directions are inserted
to display temperature development throughout the simulation. Furthermore, temperature
slice files are placed between the two facades, and on the exterior side of both facades.
Boundary files (BNDF) are applied for ‘NET HEAT FLUX’ and ‘WALL TEMPERATURE’, mak­
ing it possible to visualize the heat flux on the radiation exposed facade and the wall temper­
ature on the facade cladding.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
39
==PAGE:52==
Surface devices to measure net heat flux are placed on the radiation exposed facade directly
in front of the ignition source. Point devices (DEVC) with QUANTITY= ’THERMOCOUPLE’
are placed on the front of the radiation exposed facade, to observe the temperatures which
it is exposed to. Thermocouples are used instead of temperature as quantity, to compare
FDS results from the small model, the large two facade model and the experiment test 1.
40
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:53==
5
Preliminary Safety Distance Calculations
Two methods to calculate a safety distance for two adjacent buildings with wooden facades
have been used. The first method used is from the SFPE Handbook chapter 86 ­ The Building
Envelope: Fire Spread, Construction Features and Loss Examples. The other approach
is by using theory described by Ulf Wickström in Temperature Calculation in Fire Safety
Engineering, regarding “Radiation from flames and smoke”. An important assumption, which
is used in both methods, is that since the facade, which is on fire, is of a combustible material,
the whole facade is included as a radiator.
To support the hand calculations, and estimate which method is most suitable, three rough
FDS models have been made. For the hand calculations and FDS models the geometry
from the facades modelled in the up­scaling of the FDS model is used. Each facade has the
dimensions of 15 m in width and 17 m of height. The thickness of the facade cladding is 21
mm, but in FDS due to meshes is 50 mm. Both horizontal and vertical wooden battens are
each 50 mm x 50 mm, so the gathered width of combustible facade is 0.15 m
This section covers the results of the calculations, for full calculations, see appendix J
5.1
SFPE
The heat release rate is determined from table A.3 in literature [73]. This table is for obtaining
the theoretical unit of heat release rate for fuels burning in the open. However this table only
covers buildings with a height up to 3.7 m. With data from table A.3 in [73], a linear fitting
was done, and a heat release rate was determined to be 10477.73kW/m per 0.15 m of width.
Then Delichatsios correlation for rectangular wall flames (Hf = 0.05 · Q2/3
w ) is used to obtain
a flame height on 23.94 m. This is 6.94 meter above the top of the building. To account for
the linear fitting, other literature are being compared to this value. It is more common to see
flames about 5 m above a window opening [16]. Therefore the heat release rate are being
adjusted, so the height of flames does not exceed 22 m. This results with a heat release rate
on 9233.3 kW/m per 0.15 m of width. The emissive power is the radiant heat release rate
per unit flame surface, which is
Ep = χradpine · Qw
Hf
= 88.11
[kW/m²]
(5.1)
The value is higher than if one used known flame temperature and an emissivity of unity, that
can be calculated the following way if one assumes the lowest expected flame temperature
on 827°C (1100 K) [19].
E = σ · (273 + Te)4 = 83.01
[kW/m²]
(5.2)
With equation 5.3 used for the configuration factor, which is for a parallel rectangular radiator
and equation 5.4 for irradiance, it is possible to determine a safety distance on 25.6 m, where
the exposed surface receive an irradiance on less than 33.5 kW/m². A, B, and C are the same
as in figure 3.2.
F12 = 2

5.2
Radiation from Flame and Smoke
Determining the radiation from flames is based on using the formular for emissivity, which
includes the mean beam length. The formular for emissivity:
εfl = 1 −e−K·Le
[−]
(5.5)
The mean beam length is:
Le = L0C
(5.6)
Where L0 is the geometric mean beam length. The expressions for the Geometric mean
beam length and C ­ correction factor, are found in the SFPE Handbook, chapter 4 ­ Radiation
Heat Transfer. The whole facade that is on fire, is the beam length, and is in this case seen
as an Infinite Slab, hence the mean beam length is determined to be 39.6. For wooden
cribs, according to Temperature Calculation in Fire Safety Engineering [74], K is 0.8 m­1.
With these values known the emissivity is calculated to be 1. Assuming, as in the SFPE
calculation, that the flame temperature is 1100 K, the incident radiation can be calculated to
be 83 kW/m² using equation 5.7
qemi,fl = (1 −e−K·Le) · σ · T 4
fl
[kW/m²]
(5.7)
The incident heat flux can be determined when the configuration factor is found. Here the
method for determining the configuration factor is from Temperature Calculation in Fire Safety
Engineering [74], where the configuration factor from the wall can be divided into four equal
contributions. Therefore is the following equation multiplied with four, so the contribution
from the whole wall is accounted for. A,B and C are the same as in figure 3.2.
F12 = 4 ·
( 1
2π
[
X
√
1 + X2 · tan−1
Y
√
1 + X2 +
Y
√
1 + Y 2 · tan−1
X
√
1 + Y 2
])
(5.8)
X = A
2C
and
Y = B
2C
The equation for the incident heat flux is given in equation 5.9, and with equation 5.9 and
5.8, the safety distance has been determined to be 12.2 mm which causes an incident heat
flux on 33.3 kW/m² and a configuration factor on 0.4.
qinc = qemi,fl · F12
[kW/m²]
(5.9)

Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:55==
5.3
Rough FDS models
Three rough FDS models have been prepared, based on the model for two facades with a
distance on 10 m between them, described in section 4.3. In the models one of the facades
have been made into a radiator, where the whole facade is 1100 K, and radiating toward the
wooden facade. The radiator is non­combustible. Modelling a whole facade as a radiator, it
done to simulate the whole facade being ablaze. The models are rough, but they are made to
investigate approximately how long it takes for the radiation exposed facade to ignite, when
the wooden cladding has an ignition temperature on 375°C. The results from the simulations
are only based on visualizations and temperatures. It would have been ideal to investigate
the heat flux on the exposed wall, but a segmentation error occurred with the Linux server.
Three models were simulated. The difference in the models is the distance between the
1100 K hot radiator and the radiation exposed facade. The distances used were 10 m, 12.2
m, and 25.6 m. Where the 12.2 m and 25.6 m are the safety distances calculated above.
The 10 m case is created to be able to observe and compare the influence different ignition
sources, such as the two used in section 4.3.2, and a whole radiating facade can have.
It was observed in the FDS simulation with a distance on 10 m, that the facade cladding
exposed to heat ignited after 491 s. This is displayed in figure 5.1, TL. The fire on the facade
spread quickly after ignition. 65 s after the facade had been ignited, the whole facade is
engulfed in flames, see figure 5.1 TR. After ignition, the facade reached a temperature on
660°C. For a period from 122 s to ignition, the facade had a temperature ranging between
70°C ­ 80°C.
Figure 5.1: Facade 10 m in front of radiator. TL: Facade ignited after 491 s of radiation exposure.
TR: Facade fully ablaze after 556 s of radiation exposure.
The simulation with a safety distance on 12.2 m, were computed for 3600 s. The radiation
exposed facade did not ignite after an hour of exposure. The maximum temperature mea­
sured on the facade was 75°C, this is displayed in figure 5.2, TL. The simulation where there
was a safety distance on 25.6 m did not ignite either. The maximum temperature measured
on the radiation exposed facade was 29.5°C, this is displayed in figure 5.2, TR.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
43
==PAGE:56==
Figure 5.2: Temperature slice files on radiation exposed facades. TL: FDS simulation with a 12.2 m
safety distance. TR: FDS simulation with a 25.6 m safety distance.
5.4
Discussion
There is a large difference in the results from the safety distance calculations. With the SFPE,
chapter 86 method the safety distance with the present conditions, had to be 25.6 m. The
method with radiation from smoke and flames showed that a safety distance on 12.2 m was
necessary. The results depend greatly on the configuration factor for the two calculations.
Where for the ten­meter distance case the SFPE, chapter 86 had a configuration factor on
0.79 and an irradiance on 69.64 kW/m². With calculations from flame radiation, the ten­meter
case had a configuration factor on 0.496 and an incident heat flux on 41.15kW/m².
In the appendix of the SFPE handbook formulars for different configuration factors are listed.
From the appendix the configuration equation for rectangular parallel plates, was used on
the ten­meter distance case. This was done to find an equation, that could be used on both
methods, or find a way to calculate a configuration factor, that would resemble one of the
already calculated configuration factors. However, it was discovered, that there are limits to
the use of this equation. When the distance exceeds the length of one of the sides a or b,
the configuration factor increases, which is not how it works in the actual world.
But using the configuration factor from the SFPE appendix, within it is limits, gives a config­
uration factor on 0.47 for the ten­meter case. Which is a lot closer to the calculations from
Flame Radiation than of the SFPE chapter 86 method. Table 5.1 displays the configuration
factors for the ten­meter case, for each of the methods used
Table 5.1: Configuration factor for a distance on 10 m between facades
Method
SFPE chapter 86
Flame Radiation
SFPE appendix
Configuration factor
0.79
0.496
0.47
Here it is the Flame Radiation and SFPE appendix that are most similar. Which makes the
Flame Radiation safety distance, on 12.2 m most plausible. Furthermore, the Danish building
legislation recommends for this type of wall exterior, that there be a 10 m safety distance [9].
The rough FDS simulations support this too. Where a 10 m safety distance was not sufficient
to prevent fire spread, but 12.2 m was safe to use. Therefore, it was shown that a safety
distance on 25.6 m definitely was a safe distance to avoid fire spread, however it is overkill
to use such a large safety distance.
44
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:57==
6
Experimental Investigations
This section covers the results from the experiments performed in DTU’s FireLab. There
have been conducted four facade burning experiments, and nine bomb calorimeter tests. For
facade burning experiments the most relevant temperature graphs and figures are presented
in this section. For graphs displaying the temperature development for all thermocouples
during the experiments, see appendix F.
6.1
Results: Facade burning
6.1.1
Test 1
With facade burning test 1, there was no wind, and the ignition source had a HRR on 9.5
kW. 27 seconds after igniting the ignition source, the facade cladding had been ignited. The
flames spread to the facade and the cavity. After the ignition source had been burning for 4
minutes and 31 seconds, it was removed. This was done, due to an intense chimney effect in
the cavity, that lead to flames reaching above the construction and touching the steel hood. In
figure 6.1 the thermocouples in the two first columns nearest the fire developed in the cavity
and on the facade are showed in a graph. It can be observed that the highest temperature
does not occur closest to the ignition source, but at L1P21, which is in the cavity and 1.2 m
above the ignition source. This can be due the back of the facade cladding being on fire, and
hence contributing to the heat release and temperature increase, and due to convection the
heat rises up.
Figure 6.1: Thermocouples 2 columns closest to fire in test 1
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
45
==PAGE:58==
The temperature in the cavity is higher than on the facade. This can be due to the chimney
effect, even though the cavity is ventilated, the heat cannot escape, as it would be able to on
the front of the facade. The heat intensifies, and more of the back of the cladding is ignited,
hence the fire in the cavity is more intense, than the fire on the front of the facade. In figure
6.2, the facade cladding is displayed after the fire was extinguished. The picture to the left
is of the front of the facade, where charring is visible up to 80 cm from the bottom. On the
picture to the right, the back of the facade cladding is displayed. Here charring has occurred
from bottom to top, clearly showing the chimney effect.
Figure 6.2: Charring on the facade cladding in test 1. TL: Front of facade. TR: Back of the facade.
Figure 6.3: Thermocouples in layer 3(L3) and 4(L4)
46
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:59==
Test 1 was the only facade burning test with thermocouples placed relatively 60 cm and
120 cm in front of the facade. This was to measure how much heat radiated from the test.
Figure 6.3 displays the temperature development during the experiment. In layer 3, the
temperature was higher than in layer 4. This was a given since layer 3 was closer to the
fire. The largest temperature increase was in L3P11, which was nearest the fire, where the
temperature increased with 14°C. The temperature had a steady increase, until the ignition
source was removed.
In figure 6.4 to the left, dots in the same colors as the graphs in the diagram in figure 6.4
to the right have been placed. The dots represent the placement of the thermocouples,
which temperature development are displayed in the diagram to the right. In the diagram
measurements from the thermocouples in the cavity are included. The measurement data
used in the diagram, is at the time 4.31 min. When the ignition source was removed, and
before the temperature in layer 3 and 4 started to decrease again. There was a drastic
decrease in temperature from cavity to 120 cm in front of the facade, for point 1.1 and point
2.1, which were the points above the fire.
Figure 6.4: TL: Coloured dots representing thermocouples in test 1. TR: Temperature development
from cavity to layer 4.
6.1.2
Test 2
During facade burning test 2, there was wind with variating speed, and the ignition source had
a HRR on 9.5 kW. A horizontal gypsum plate was added to the top of the facade construction,
to prolong the time before the flames reached the steel hood. This also increased the time
the facade was affected by the ignition source. The side of the hood opposite the wind source
was drawn, to protect the rest of the lab from flying embers and keep the smoke under the
hood.
In table 6.1, are the wind speed, and when it was turn on and off listed. The time is time from
when the ignition source was ignited.

From the graph in figure 6.5 it can be observed that during the wind breaks, the heat in­
creased. In the periods where there was no wind, the fire grew. When the wind was applied
the temperature decreased, and the fire in the facade was kept down and close to being
extinguished.
Figure 6.5: Thermocouples 2 columns closest to fire in test 2
In figure 6.6, the heat flux measured once every 60th second, is displayed. The heat flux
pattern follows the temperature pattern. When there was no wind affecting the facade the
heat flux increased, and decreased when wind was applied, and when the heat source was
gone.
Figure 6.6: Heat flux development for test 2
In test 2 the ignition source was not removed, due to the flames not spreading up over the
wall construction. This was clear from the charring displayed in figure 6.7 to the right. The
charring on the back of the facade cladding has only spread up to 80 cm. The bottom of the
facade was influenced by the ignition source for 7.42 minutes.

==PAGE:61==
Figure 6.7: Charring on the facade cladding in test 2. TL: Front of facade. TR: Back of the facade.
6.1.3
Test 3
In facade burning test 3, the ignition source had a HRR on 9.5 kW, and the facade was
exposed to wind, with a speed of 3.5 m/s 5.40 minutes after ignition of the ignition source.
The wind was then after 1 minute increased to 5 m/s and kept constant. The wind was first
applied after 5.40 minutes to make sure that the fire was burning steady and had ignited the
facade cladding. Figure 6.8 shows the thermocouples closets to the fire. There was a steady
temperature growth for the points L1P11, L1P21 and L2P11 until the facade was exposed to
wind. Then the temperature started to increase. However, for the points L1P22, L2P12 and
L2P22 the temperature had a steep increase, as soon as the wind machine was turned on to
3.5 m/s, this could be due to wind blowing the heat in the direction of the thermocouples. The
temperature for the aforementioned thermocouples decreased again when the wind speed
was increased to 5 m/s.
Figure 6.8: Thermocouples 2 columns closest to fire in test 3
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
49
==PAGE:62==
The heat flux evolution is shown in figure 6.9. The heat flux peaks right before the facade is
exposed to wind, and then it steadily decreases, as the wind speed increases. The evolution
of the heat flux does not follow the temperature graph. This can be explained by the heat
flux was measured and noted once every minute, whereas the temperature from the ther­
mocouples was measured every second. The heat flux measurement is coarser, and hence
the increase and decrease over a shorter period can be difficult to catch.
Figure 6.9: Heat flux development for test 3
The bottom of the facade cladding was exposed to the ignition source for 8.16 minutes until
there was no methanol left. This led to almost 15x15 cm² burned away. In figure 6.10 to the
right the charring is showed on the back of the cladding. Here it is clear that the fire on the
facade had time to grow and ignite the back of the cladding, since there is charring all the
way to the top of the facade cladding. There is a lighter charring to the right of the fire on
the facade, at a place, where there has not been charring in the other tests. This indicates
that a steady fire exposed to wind will lead to more flame spread. This also corresponds to
figure 6.8, where there is an increase in temperature for three thermocouples, when the wind
machine is applied.
Figure 6.10: Charring on the facade cladding in test 3. TL: Front of facade. TR: Back of the facade.
50
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:63==
6.1.4
Test 4
The ignition source in test 4 was increased. In the first three test one pan with a diameter on
24 cm was used. In this test two pans were added, with a diameter on relatively 20 cm and
28 cm. Each pan was filled with 204g of methanol. Together the three pans contributed to a
ignition source on 29 kW. 2.31 minutes after ignition, the fire on the facade was steady and
strong enough to apply the wind source. A wind speed on 2 m/s was used. After 5.39 min­
utes the flames coming out from the top of the wall construction were too high and touching
the steel hood. Therefore the ignition source (the pans) was removed, and water extinction
started. Figure 6.11 displays the temperature development during the short test. The tem­
perature increased much faster than the previous tests. However, given that, the ignition
source was much larger, this was expected.
Figure 6.11: Thermocouples 2 columns closest to fire in test 4
In figure 6.12 the heat flux for test 4 is displayed. The heat flux is peaking at 920 W/m² one
meter away from the fire, right as the facade is exposed to wind. The heat flux decreases as
the pans either run out of fuel or is removed, thus visible flames on the facade disappears,
and do not radiate heat. There are few data points for the heat flux in this test compared
to the prior tests. This is because the fire needed to be extinguished, since the fire was too
large to be safe at the FireLab.
Figure 6.12: Heat flux development for test 4
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
51
==PAGE:64==
From figure 6.13 the charring on the front and back of the facade cladding is displayed. The
charring on the front, has at its highest reached 70 cm, and there is only charging above the
ignition source. On the back of the facade cladding, charring has spread to the top of the
cladding, and is not only above the ignition source.
Figure 6.13: Charring on the facade cladding in test 4. TL: Front of facade. TR: Back of the facade.
6.1.5
Validation of Thermocouples
During the facade burning experiments, a thermal camera was used to take thermal images
of the facade and do point measures of temperature. The pictures and temperature mea­
surements taken with FLIR TG165, have been used to validate the thermocouples’ measure­
ments.
In figure 6.14, thermal pictures with temperature measurements, are shown for, what is es­
timated to be relatively 2.15 min and 3.15 min into facade test 1. Furthermore, the fire at the
given time, is shown next to the thermal picture.
Figure 6.14: TL: Thermal picture and temperature on the facade point L2P11 at 2.15 min, and a
picture of the fire. TR: Thermal picture and temperature on the facade between point L2P21 and
L2P22 at 3.15 min, and a picture of the fire.
The precise time­step of the thermal pictures taken, has not been noted. However, based
on the recordings of the experiment test 1, and that the thermal camera was used after
52
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:65==
measurements was noted for the HT­meter, the time of the pictures have been estimated.
Furthermore, to account for the insecurities of the time­step of picture taken, the thermocou­
ple measurements have been averaged over a 11 second time­span, with 5 seconds before
and after the estimated time­step for the thermal picture.
The thermal picture shown to the left in figure 6.14, shows a point temperature of 380.7°C, at
what corresponds to the thermocouple placed on the facade at point 1.1 (L2P11). The L2P11
thermocouple has an average temperature on 378.5°C at the corresponding time­step. This
is a 0.6% difference between the thermocouple and thermal camera, corresponding to 2.2°C.
For the thermal picture to the right in figure 6.14, the point temperature measured is 68.8°C.
The point of measurement corresponds to a point right in between the thermocouples L2P21
and L2P22. To determine the temperature between the two thermocouples, linear interpo­
lation was used. Afterwards the temperatures have been averaged over an 11 second time
period, and resulted in a temperature on 70.3°C. This is a 2.2°C temperature difference, cor­
responding to a 3.1% difference between thermocouples and thermal camera. As clarified
in section 4.1.1, the accuracy of thermocouples type K is +/­ 2.2°C or +/­ 0.75%, depend­
ing on which one is greater. This validates the accuracy of the thermocouples, since both
measurements are within this range.
6.1.6
Error Sources
During the facade burning tests error sources occurred.
• The thermocouples at point L2P26, placed on the facade in the topmost left corner,
turned out to be faulty, and could only register a temperature on ­9.90·1037°C. Ther­
mocouple XX, which was placed in the cavity, but right above the ignition source (in test
1­3), did not register any temperature higher than 25°C. Given that the thermocouples
placed above XX measured temperatures much higher, thermocouple XX was deemed
faulty too.
• The gypsum used as the back of the wall construction, was covered a thin layer of
paper. This paper was burned away during test 1 and can have contributed a little to
the heat release in the beginning of the fire. The back gypsum plate was not changed
between the tests, and hence did not contribute to the fire in test 2­4.
• The gypsum plate, placed horizontal on top of the wall construction, shown in figure
6.15, was added to the construction after test 1. This was done to prolong the time the
facade cladding could be exposed to the ignition source before flames started touching
the steel hood. This was a change, which increased the time the facade was exposed
for the ignition source for the later tests. This change makes charring and burned away
cladding difficult to compare within the four tests.
Figure 6.15: Gypsum plate on top of wall construction.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
53
==PAGE:66==
• The size of the facade burning experiments were too large for the facilities of FireLab,
and hence test 1 and 4 were stopped premature. Where in test 1, the ignition source
was removed, and after some time the fire died down. With test 4, one pan had to be
removed, and water extinction was used to kill the fire. In these two tests, it was not
possible to observe the full extent and potential of the fire and flame spreading.
6.1.7
Result Summation
From the facade burning tests it can be concluded that wind influences how the flame spread
behave. Where it was notable from charring, and temperature moving in the same direction
as the wind direction. In table 6.2 the temperature from the thermocouples in the cavity and
on the facade are displayed from test 1 and 3. This was a test with no wind influence, and
the same test with a constant wind applied after 5.40 minutes. From the table it can be
observed that when comparing test 1 with test 3, the thermocouples father away from the
fire registered a higher maximum temperature, with wind applied. For the test without wind,
the higher temperatures are concentrated near and above the fire from ignition source, and
fire on the facade.

It was also observed, that if the wind was activated before the fire on the facade had grown
strong, the wind would repress the fire. This was especially clear from test 2, where the
temperature and heat flux decreased when wind was applied and increased again when it
was off. Of the four tests, the highest temperature observed was in test 1 (888.4°C), where
there was no wind, implying that wind can contribute to cooling of the fire too.

ignition source was increased, and more than doubled compared to previous tests. The part
of facade cladding exposed to the ignition source, was by this measure also increased. In
table 6.3 temperatures from test 1 and 4 are displayed. From the table it can be observed
that the overall temperature in the cavity increases significantly. However, the maximum
temperature in test 4 does not exceed the maximum temperature in the test 1, which can be

highest peak heat flux is reached at 920 W/m². Compared to test 2 and 3, it is considerably

6.2
Discussion
The size of the ignition source used in the tests, 9.5 kW, was rather small compared to test
standards. Test standard 13785 uses an ignition source on 100 kW, from a sandbox burner
with propane as fuel [66]. As mentioned in section 4.1.1, the fuel source was chosen to be
small due to safety reasons. Had an ignition source with a HRR on 100 kW been used,
flames would have engulfed the facade, and the flames would have damaged the steel hood
and exhaust system. The exhaust system would not have been able to keep up with the
smoke production, which could potentially cause personal and property damage. However,
an ignition source with a HRR on 100 kW is more realistic to occur in the real world. According
to Enclosure Fire Dynamics, by Karlsson and Quintiere [22] this corresponds to the energy
release from a waste basket. It would be realistic to assume this size of fire could occur by a
high­rise building, either by some rubbish by the bottom, or from a balcony with a barbeque.
It would have been interesting to observe how the fire on the facade would have propagated,
had it been able to use all the fuel. Flames might have spread on the front of the facade,
since the wood would have more time to heat up and make the conditions more ideal for
flame spread.
The construction of the facade, with open sides into the void behind the facade cladding is
not ordinary building practice. The sides are in general closed off, and then the void is either
fully ventilated, partly ventilated or closed off at top and bottom, as described in figure 2.2.
Additionally, it is according to Danish regulations not permitted to build with both horizontal
and vertical wooden battens in buildings with floors above 22 meter. For buildings with top
floor placed 22 m above ground or less, the 10% rule applies, where only 10% of the exposed
surface of the suspension system can be in material class D­s2,d2 [9]. There where lots of
oxygen present, due to the way the facade was constructed. The chimney effect occurred
in experiment 1, 3, and 4. The chimney effect was anticipated for the experiments, but not
in the extent that occurred. The large chimney effect can have occurred since the fire in the
cavity had optimal conditions. There were great access to both fuel, the facade cladding,
and oxygen coming from the side openings. Even though the large extent of the chimney
effect was not anticipated, it came to have a great influence on the experiments. Due to
the chimeny effect the wall construction had to be altered, and some of the experiments
shortened. The chimney effect showed that it is very important to think of fire stop, and other
ways to prevent rapid fire spread in the cavity. Furthermore this can explain how some of
the facade fires from section 2.4 spread as much as they did.
The wind source was directed onto the facade surface at a 45­degree angle. This impacted
the ignition source and the fire on the front of the facade, however as mentioned in the results,
it also aided in the flame spreading. Had the wind source been placed with a 0­degree angle
to the facade, the wind would have blown directly into the void. This would probably had
contributed to more horizontal flame­, and temperature spread in the flow direction of the
wind. As previously described the sides are closed off in common building practice. When
this is done, wind from the side will not have as great an influence on the horizontal flame
and temperature spread in the void. If the void is ventilated, or partially ventilated, wind has
access from the bottom of the facade, and the wind can aid the fire in the vertical direction
[23].
Given that on the front of the facade there were not much flame and flame spread compared
to the cavity, the thermocouples in layer 3 and 4 should have been placed lower and closer to
the fire. This would have given a higher temperature increase, and the measurement would
have been more interesting to observe. However, these measurements also show that there
is not a large temperature increase 60­120 cm away from the fire, which for fire safety and
spread to other buildings is a positive result.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
55
==PAGE:68==
6.3
Results: Bomb Calorimeter
The bomb calorimeter tests, as described in section 4.1.2, were carried out with the wooden
battens, frame, and facade cladding as test species. The results from each of the test are
displayed in this section. For each of the three test types, a graph, showing the temperature
development, and a table, with the complete heat of combustion, have been created.
Each graph starts at the 180 second mark. This is because the first 180 seconds are used
for the IKA C2000 bomb calorimeter to equalize. After the 180 seconds, the main test starts,
here the test specie is ignited, and one will observe a temperature increase.
Wooden Battens
In figure 6.16 and table 6.4 the temperature development and heat of combustion results
for the three tests with the wooden battens are shown. The temperature increase follows a
logarithmic curve, where there is a fast increase of temperature in the beginning, and then
the increase slows down. The average heat of combustion for the three tests was determined
to be 23.52 MJ/kg.

Summary
It was observed that after each test, the whole test specie was combusted. Furthermore,
no residue was left back in the crucible. On the bottom of the cover of the decomposition
vessel, small droplets of water were found.
All tests conducted with the bomb calorimeter have been made with pinewood, but each have
been treated differently. The wooden battens have been pressure impregnated, the facade
cladding has been thermos treated, so the moisture content has been decreased, and the
frame has only been planed. As can be observed from table 6.7 the heat of combustion
varies between 20.91 and 23.52 MJ/kg. Comparing this with values found in literature, the
heat of combustion found in the bomb calorimeter test are higher. In the SFPE handbook
the heat of combustion value for Ponderosa pine is 19.4 MJ/kg [19, p. 143] and for Wood
(pine) the heat of combustion is 17.9 MJ/kg [19, p. 3467]. However just observing the values
from the SFPE handbook, the values for pine varies. Other literature mentions that the heat
of combustion for wood is about 15­20 MJ/kg [13, p. 68]. It is then noted that the bomb
calorimeter tests have given higher values, than the literature. Both the frame and facade
cladding are somewhat close to the high end of the literature values. It could be that the
wooden battens have been treated with pressure impregnation, and hence contributing to a
higher heat of combustion value.

6.3.2
Uncertainties
As mentioned in the section above, the measured values for pine does not correspond to the
literature. There can occur some possible errors during the bomb calorimeter experiments,
that could affect the results. The weight of each test specie can influence the results. The
measured mass could be more precise with one more decimal. The scale available in FireLab
had one decimal less than what could be written into the bomb calorimeter display/prepare
menu. Hence the heat of combustion values could have been more accurate. The cleaning of
the decomposition vessel and crucible can influence the results too. The vessel and crucible
were cleaned between each test, however the inside of the vessel, had some dirt, that was
not possible to remove.

figures are too displayed in this section.

drastic compared to the simulation, where there is a big temperature drop.

(4.31 minutes mark), one minute after the ignition source is removed (5.31 minutes) and when
no flaming is visible at the front of the facade and the fire on the facade had self­extinguished
(11.31 minutes).
At the time step 4.26 minutes, the flame height on the front, is in the experiment 60 cm up,
whereas in the FDS simulation the flame height is 55 cm. In the cavity in the experiment the
flames are already reaching the top of the facade, where in the FDS simulation, the flames
have only reached up 120 cm. One minute after the ignition source has been removed there
are only small non­continuous flames on the front of the facade in the experiment. In the

cm. At the time when the fire on the facade had extinguished in the experiment, the FDS
simulation displayed flames 30 cm above the top of the facade cladding, and 35 cm flames
from the bottom on the front of the facade.
