7.3
Discussion
It is important when doing FDS simulations, to be aware of pitfalls, errors and where uncer­
tainties can occur. Professor Phillip Rubini1 has once said:
”A report of a CFD simulation without discussion of error is of little value”
Hence this section covers issues to be aware of, when applying this model, errors and where
reality and simulation might not compare.
7.3.1
Iteration Process
Multiple simulations have been run, to adjust and make the model as true to the real world
as possible. However, one has to be careful not to change too much. If one must change
too much in the model, it is not a good model to explain the real physical conditions.
The parameters that have been changed between the different simulations have been: Igni­
tion temperature, number of radiation angels and HRR of the ignition source.
There have been run simulations with different ignition temperatures, since different sources
have determined different ignition temperatures for pine. By the last simulations it was found
that the ignition temperature, that had the most similar temperature development as the ex­
periment was 375°C. As mentioned in section 3.2.1, the ignition temperature varies between
349­446°C depending on the source used. Alternatively, the ignition temperature could have
been determined with a TGA­analysis (Thermogravimetric analysis) in the FireLab.
Simulations were run with 100 and 500 radiation angles. Where it was clear that the most re­
alistic results came from the simulations with 500 radiation angles. One should in simulations
validating experiments, measuring radiation from flames to building parts, and verification of
flame spread increase the number of radiation angles to 500. Increasing the number of radi­
ation angles increases the computational time, hence why it was tested with the default 100
radiations angles. The final simulation was run with 500 radiation angles.
The HRR of the ignition source, was the parameter changed the most. In the experiment in
the FireLab an ignition source with a HRR on 9.5 kW was used. Running simulations with
a 9.5 kW HRR brought temperatures that were nowhere near the ones measured in the lab.
1Professor Philip Rubini. CEng, FIChemE, MIET. University of Hull, UK [76]
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
65
==PAGE:78==
It was found, that if the HRR was increased to 14 kW, the same temperatures as in the lab
were reached. The large difference in HRR can be due to that fact, that FDS is not optimal
for solving conductivity. This is explained in more detail in section 7.3.3.
7.3.2
Flame Spread
In the simulation, the flames are present during the whole simulation, and does not extinguish
as they do in the experimental test. This can be due to the fact, that there is no coupling
between the burning rate and surface temperature in FDS. Which can lead to a too large
increase in surface temperature. Since there is a higher surface temperature, the conditions
for flame spread, and a continuous flames in the cavity are ideal. In the experiment the
flames extinguish in the cavity 3.33 minutes after removal of ignition source. The last flames
on the front of the facade are observed 5.35 minutes after removal of ignition source. Here
the Ts,min (temperature outside the immediate range of flames, in this case, the temperature
of the facade cladding) is too low for the flames to continue. As far as the writer knows,
there is not a Ts,min or similar, that can be added as either a material­ or surface parameter
in FDS. However, another approach to the model could have been to use AUTO_IGNITION
on the REAC line, and not used the IGNITION_TEMPERATURE as a surface parameter.
Then REACTION_TEMPERATURE could have been added to the thermal properties of the
material. The reaction temperature is typically found with a TGA­analysis. This approach
has not been used, since it is a bit more complex, and not enough time to redo the model
was available.
7.3.3
Other Errors and Uncertainties
FDS is originally made for fluids and not solids. FDS uses FVM (Finite Volume Method), and
can hence solve for fluids fairly successful. However when it comes to solids, it is much more
appropriate to use FEM (Finite Element Method). Heat conduction takes place in a solid, and
is then best solved with FEM. The default solver for conduction is the 1D heat conduction
solver, and the 3D solver is still in BETA testing. This can lead to the heat conduction not
being solved just as good as radiation and convection. This can be of influence on the results
for the heat, since convection and radiation from the cavity are not being lead by conduction
into the gypsum layer behind it.
One should for FDS simulations conduct grid analysis, to check for grid convergence. This
has not been done here. It is most common to observe weak convergence for larger models
with coarse grids. In this simulation a grid resolution near the fire was on 0.02³m, and then
in areas not too relevant 0.1³ m. This was deemed to be a fine grid, and hence there should
be grid convergence.
Physical instabilities can occur in FDS simulations. This tends to occur near changes in
cell sizes, where there is a sudden change in obstructions or heat release rate, and in fully
closed enclosures. The model has been checked for any physical abnormalities by checking
the velocity vector slices near the fire and the exhaust fan, here no abnormalities occurred.
The velocity vector flow was as expected; flowing upwards from the fire to the exhaust fan.
However, the model should have been checked with pressure slice files, but it was assumed
that given the model is open, there would not occur pressure instabilities. Had there been
issues with pressure, it would have been noted from the flow of velocity.
7.3.4
Up-Scaling
The FDS model is used moving forward with up­scaling it to simulate a fire on a high rise
building adjacent to another high­rise building. It is deemed appropriate to do so, because
the FDS model is conservative compared to the experiment, with exemption from the effect
from the HRR from the ignition source. The HRR must be higher in the model to generate
temperature and heat flux results that are comparable to the experiment. However, the flame
66
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:79==
development follows the experiment until the ignition source is removed, then flame spread
is more extensive in the FDS model than in the experiment. In a bigger model the flame will
probably be more extensive, since a bigger ignition source is used. There will be more flame
spread, and a greater fire on the facade will evolve than what will happen in reality, hence
making this parameter of the model conservative. When scaling up the model, one should
be attentive on the heat release rate. Discussion on the up­scaled FDS­models is found in
section 8.2.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
67
==PAGE:80==
8
Up-Scaling of FDS-Model
This section contains results and discussion of six FDS simulations, with three different dis­
tances between facades and two fire scenarios. The FDS models are described previously
in section 4.3. The results are displayed in graphs and visualizations of the simulations.
8.1
Results
The simulations completed for this section, were supposed to simulate 3600 s (one hour).
However it has not been possible to compute the simulation long enough, due to time limita­
tions and available computational power. Additionally, each of the models have not simulated
the same amount of time, therefore the amount of data for the results for the different cases
will not be same.
8.1.1
Temperature
The thermocouples were placed on the facade which was exposed to radiation, however,
there has been a problem with the placement of the thermocouples, because they do not
display any change in temperature. This can be caused by the model seeing the placement of
the thermocouples in the cladding, and not in front of the cladding. To get some temperature
measurements, a temperature slice was placed 5 mm in front of the facade exposed to
radiation. The maximum temperatures are found for fewer time­steps with the use of slice
files instead of the data. In figure 8.1 is a visualization of a temperature slice file just in front
of the radiation exposed facade.
Figure 8.1: Visualization of temperature slice on radiation exposed facade.
In figure 8.2 the maximum temperature at each timestep is displayed for each of the six cases
simulated. There are three simulations with a waste basket igniting the facade at the bottom,
one for relatively 5 m, 7.5 m, and 10 m distance between the facades. The same distances
have been used for three simulations with flames coming out of two windows on the first
floor.
68
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:81==
Figure 8.2: Maximum temperature on the facade of the radiation exposed facade, for each simulation.
It can be seen from figure 8.2, that the temperature on the radiation exposed facade is larger
in the simulations with the ignition source coming from the windows than compared to the
simulations with the ignition source being a wastebasket. Furthermore, the temperature is
higher, when the distance is 5 m than compared to the 7.5 m and 10 m.
In figure 8.3 a slice file in the 10 m case with a waste basket is displayed. Here it is notable,
that the temperature from the burning facade toward the radiation exposed facade is not
increasing significantly, which pairs well with the results from figure 8.2.
Figure 8.3: Temperature slice file in between two facades, with 10 m distance, for the case with a
waste basket as fire scenario
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
69
==PAGE:82==
8.1.2
Heat Flux
The net heat flux received on the wall adjacent to the fire have been measured. The net
heat flux is the sum of emitted and absorbed radiation on the solid surface of the facade. In
this case the facade cladding, which is adjacent to the fire, is the solid surface where heat
flux is measured. In figure 8.4 the heat flux received on the radiation exposed facade for
scenario SC11, SC21, and SC31 are displayed. Scenarios ending with a “1”, are the cases
with fire scenarios with a waste basket as ignition source. Where scenarios ending with “2”
are fire scenarios with the ignition source being flames from two windows in a compartment
influenced by the occurrence of a flashover. Figure 8.5 displays the heat flux received by the
radiation exposed facade for the fire scenarios SC12, SC22, and SC32.
Figure 8.4: Heat flux received on the radiation exposed facade for the fire cases with a waste basket
as ignition source
Figure 8.5: Heat flux received on the radiation exposed facade for the fire cases with a flashover
compartment with two windows, as ignition source.
At the 300 s, 450 s, and 600 s mark, there are large dips in the measurements. These
dips occur at the time, where the model have been restarted. The dips are not caused by
a change in ignition source or ambient conditions. They are a computational error from the
restart, hence the dips should just be disregarded.
70
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:83==
8.1.3
Flame Spread
For the fire cases with a waste basket, as an ignition source the following were observed:
• The same chimney effect as with the experiment and the FDS simulation of the exper­
iment. The ignition source was placed by the bottom of the facade and had access to
the cavity in the facade. The flame spread in the cavity was large enough to extend
out and above the facade. This is pictured in figure 8.7 TL.
• The temperature on the radiation exposed facade, was highest at the top of the facade,
since there was more radiation from the flames on top of the facade, than compared
to the bottom, where ignition occurred.
• Not much flame spread on the front of the facade. There were a lot more flame spread
in the cavity.
• By the ignition source a small part of the facade cladding burned away. This is pictured
in figure 8.6 for two timesteps.
Figure 8.6: For fire case with waste basket as ignition source, burned away facade cladding at: TL:
710 seconds after ignition. TR: 785 seconds after ignition.
For the fire cases with flames from windows as ignition source, the following was observed:
• Flame. did not spread into the cavity, hence only flame spread on the front of the
facade. The flame spread on the facade is pictured in figure 8.7 TR.
• The highest temperature on the radiation exposed facade was observed right in the
middle, between the flaming windows.
Figure 8.7: Flame spread for the two different fire scenarios: TL: Waste basket as ignition source.
TR: Flames from two windows as ignition source.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
71
==PAGE:84==
8.1.4
Safety Distance
Based on the results from the six large scale simulations, 5 m is a safe distance to apply
between the two buildings with wooden facades. The highest heat flux on the radiation
exposed facade is less than 1 kW/m² and the highest temperature is near 21°C. Given that
the critical heat flux for piloted ignition for wood is 12.5 kW/m² and the ignition temperature
for pinewood is 375°C, ignition on the radiation exposed facade will not occur. This is within
850 seconds (∼14minutes) of the fire duration, and ignition of ignition source.
8.2
Discussion
There are three things that could have improved the simulations. The first thing is the com­
puting time of the simulations. The simulations did not reach the full 3600 s (60 min) of
simulation. As a result the simulations does not cover the full influence of the fire on the
radiation exposed building. Hence the results are only valid for at most 888 s, and anything
beyond that, are estimated and approximated. It would create better results if the simulations
covered the full 3600 seconds. Secondly the number of radiation angles are only set to 100.
Even though in section 7.3 it was concluded, that with cases where radiation from flames
was present, it would be best with number of radiation angles set to 500. The choice to use
only 100 as number of radiation angles, was made to make the models less computational
heavy, and decrease computation time. This might lead to temperatures and heat fluxes on
the radiation exposed wall a little lower, than if the number of radiation angles had been 500.
The third thing that could be improved would be the placement of the thermocouples. The
thermocouples have been placed in such a way, that they do not measure any temperature
change on the surface of the radiation exposed facade. The temperatures in figure 8.2 were
found by using a temperature slice file placed 5 mm in front of the exposed facade, which is
not optimal. It would have been better if measurements were found directly on the facade.
This would have resulted in higher and more precise temperatures. However, given that the
temperatures that were found were not higher than 21°C, it is estimated, that had it been pos­
sible the get results directly from the facade, it would still not be temperatures high enough
to ignite the facade.
Even though the simulations did not run the full amount of time, the heat flux graphs for
the fire cases with flames from windows look like they have reached a level where the heat
flux does not increase much. There is further no reason to believe that the heat flux would
increase, unless part of the facade burned away, so flames could creep into the cavity, and
there would occur flaming above the top of the facade. For the heat flux to increase there
would have to be more flame spread on the front of the facade as well. Additionally. The
heat flux from the fire cases with the waste basket did have a steep increase, the heat flux
is so low, maximum 10.88 W/m², that it would not be likely to increase 12.489 kW/m², to a
level where it would be able to ignite the radiation exposed facade. In all the fire cases the
fire and ignition source are too small to spread to an adjacent building.
In the cases with flames from windows, as ignition source, no part of the facade burned away.
This can be because the simulations did not run for long enough time. Furthermore, the
facade cladding only received fire exposure from one side, compared to the waste basket
ignition source, where the cladding burned away because it was exposed to fire on both
sides. The assumption is that, if the simulation had been running for a longer time, part of
the facade would have burnt away, and then created an opening to the cavity, and the same
chimney effect as in the waste basket scenarios would have been observed.
72
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:85==
9
Discussion
Different methods used to estimate a safety distance have been used, and the distance
have varied greatly depending on method. Some methods have been very conservative
with assumptions, like the whole facade being on fire, whereas other methods have been less
conservative and more realistic. The methods used in the section regarding the preliminary
calculations, section 5, were on the conservative side. Where in the calculations it was
assumed that the whole facade was ignited. This gave safety distances ranging from 12.2
m to 25.6 m, which is a large span. However, this was roughly modelled in FDS, where
it turned out that the 25.6 m was on the very conservative side, and that 12.2 m was an
appropriate distance, when the building is 15 m wide and 17 m tall. The building regulation’s
most conservative distance, in the pre­accepted solutions, is 10 m, but even here one cannot
use wood in the whole facade, when the top floor is placed 22 m or less above ground. Only
20 % of the facade is allowed to be in wood, when it is classified as D­s2,d2, and it has to be
evenly distributed. If the top floor is placed more than 22 m above ground, the rain cover has
to be in class A2­s1,d0 [9]. Furthermore, if one follows the performance­based requirement
there cannot be a radiated heat flux on an adjacent building/construction on more than 15
kW/m² within the first hour of the fire. This heat flux is higher than the 12.5 kW/m² for piloted
ignition for wood, and lower than the 33.5 kW/m² for spontaneous ignition. Here the 15 kW/m²
restriction will be fine, to avoid spontaneous ignition. However if the wood has been heated
up, and exposed to radiation for a while, and then an ember or brand lands on the exposed
wood, the 15 kW/m² might not be good enough to avoid a piloted ignition [19, Chapter 86].
However the chapter notes, that the 12.5 kW/m² can be on the conservative side, regarding
piloted ignition. This maybe need to be investigated further. The large FDS models based on
the up­scaling of the facade experiment, showed that a 5 m distance was safe enough avoid
fire propagation between two high­rise building with wooden facades. However, the fires on
the large facades were small compared to having the whole facade ablaze. Here the author
was too focused on the ignition sources being as realistic as possible and comply with former
experiments and literature on the topic. To have ended up with some more useful results from
the large simulations, it would have been more appropriate to have created larger ignition
sources. Where the larger ignition source should represent a case of a fire that had been
burning for a while and grown strong. The models used here, are from the beginning of a
fire. It would have been better to start the simulation further into the duration of a fire.
There are things that could have been improved, to get more credible and useful results.
The large FDS models should have contained larger ignition sources, as described prior,
to comply with the limitation section in section 1.3. The facade should already be partially
ablaze, when the simulations were started. Here the rough FDS models in the preliminary
calculations might be better to give an estimate of a safety distance, because it is a rough
estimate of a huge fire. Time could have been utilized more optimal, either on optimizing
the large FDS models, or making the rough FDS models more thorough and detailed. It
would have been beneficial if there had been more focus on using heat flux measurement,
in the rough FDS models from the preliminary calculations. The results would have been
more comparable to the heat flux requirement from the building regulations, and the ignition
heat flux from the SFPE handbook. However, a segmentation error occurred, which possible
could be a problem with Linux. It was deemed by the author, that instead of using a great
amount of time trying to work around it, that temperature slice files, were the second­best
option, with less time wasted.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
73
==PAGE:86==
10
Conclusion
There have been many facade fires in high­rise buildings throughout the last 20 years [6].
The facade fires occur in the combustible part of the facade and spreads. More wood is intro­
duced into buildings and hence increasing the amount of combustible material. Additionally,
the legislation regarding fire does not make it straightforward to use wood in buildings. Even
wood in facade cladding in high­rise building above 22 m is not permitted in the prescriptive­
based codes [9]. Therefore, this thesis was completed with the purpose to determine a
safety distance between high­rise buildings with facades of wooden material, and contribute
to work needing to be done, so the increased use of wood in buildings, can be done in a
safely manner.
The following was performed and concluded:
In the preliminary hand calculations and rough FDS models, the whole facade was consid­
ered as a radiator, i.e., the whole facade being ablaze. With the given conditions, there
should be a safety distance between two wooden facade buildings on either 12.2 m or 25.6
m depending on which method used. The SFPE, chapter 86 method resulted in a safety
distance on 25.6 m. The method using theory on radiation from flames resulted in a safety
distance on 12.2 m. This is only for buildings with dimensions on 15 m of width and 17 m of
height, and for the SFPE handbook method, a thickness on the facade cladding on 150 mm.
Rough FDS models with 10 m, 12.2 m, and 25.6 m distance between radiator and facade
were created to compare hand calculations with FDS simulations. It was found that with
these conservative assumptions, that in FDS if the distance between facades was 10 m the
radiation exposed facade would ignite after 491 seconds. It proved safe with an increased
safety distance to 12.2 m. With this distance the radiation exposed facade would not ignite
within 3600 s. The simulation was computed for 3600 s. The 12.2 m corresponds to the
hand calculations using theory on radiation from flames.
Four intermediate facade burning experiments were conducted, with different conditions
present. There was test 1, which was without wind present. Test 2 had same ignition source,
but a PPV was used as a wind source with different levels. Test 3 had the same conditions
as in test 2, but only two lower wind speeds was used. Test 4 used an ignition source more
than double HRR from test 1 and was exposed to a continuous low wind speed. The tests
demonstrated that wind and ignition source influence the flame spread and the temperature
in the cavity and on the facade. The heat flux, measured one meter from the fire on the
facade, was also affected by wind and size of ignition source. The greatest temperature
measurement was 888.4°C in the cavity in test 1. Additionally, a strong chimney effect was
observed in the tests with no wind or low wind speeds.
A FDS model was constructed to simulate the conditions from the physical experiment, test
1 with no wind applied. It was possible to create the same chimney effect, and the same
temperature patterns were also found. However, in the model used, the Ts,min was not ac­
counted for, so more persistent flame and flame spread was observed. When the fire in
the experiment extinguished, the flames in the simulation continued. It was necessary to
increase the ignition source from 9.5 kW, which was used in the physical experiment, to 14
kW. This was done so the temperatures in the cavity, and on the facade, in the simulation
were in the same range as in the physical experiment. The peak temperature in the simula­
tion, before the ignition source was removed, was found in the cavity near the ignition source
on 922.5°C. It was deemed that the results from the FDS model resembled the results from
74
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:87==
the physical test enough to use in further FDS simulations. The model was used as a basis
to construct larger models for high­rise buildings with wooden facades.
Six large FDS cases, with two different fire scenarios and three different facade distances,
5 m, 7.5 m, and 10 m, were created based on the FDS simulation made of the physical
experiment. The first fire scenario was an ignition source modelled to resemble a waste
basket, with a HRR on 100 kW. The second fire scenario was created to resemble flames
spreading out from two windows from a compartment fire. Each window had a heat release
rate per unit area on 200 kW/m². The intention was to observe the fire propagation over the
course of one hour. The models were computational heavy. The case that covered the most
amount of simulated time was the case with flames from windows as ignition source, and 5
m distance between the wooden facades. The simulation ran for 888 seconds, and no fire
spread between the two facades occurred. No fire propagation between the facades in the
other cases was observed either. From the six simulations it could be concluded that 5 m
would be a safe distance. However the fires were small, and in the beginning stages of a
fire. The full duration of the fire was not simulated, so this result should be considered with
skepticism.
The research completed in this thesis suggest that when using wooden facades, the safety
distance should be chosen with care. There is a conservative approach, that results in a 12.2
m safety distance between two wooden facades with the dimensions of 15 m x 17 m. A less
conservative method with the use of FDS suggests that for the prior mentioned dimensions,
that 5 m would be sufficient. However, this method does not consider the whole facade being
ablaze, has only been simulated for 888 s, and should be used with care, since this is not
generalized for all wooden species and facade constructions.
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
75
==PAGE:88==
11
Further Work
Small Scale CFD
For further work, it would seem logical to investigate and optimize the small scale FDS model
of test 1, in regards to the inclusion of a Ts,min.
Whether it be with a segment missing
to the software, or if the physical parameter is in some way accounted for when applying
AUTO_IGNITION_TEMPERATURE, REACTION_TEMPERATURE and other more detailed
kinetic parameters. For future validation models, it would be optimal to have a more realistic
range of flame spread, and for flames to extinguish, to comply with the real physical world.
Large Scale CFD
It would for further work with the large CFD models, make sense to set aside enough time,
more than a month, to run the models, so the results could be observed for an hour. Fur­
thermore, the model could be optimized, so it would be less computational heavy, making it
possible to compute the whole hour of the model in a shorter time span.
This model could for further works be used to investigate the wind influence on fire propaga­
tion on either one or both facades. This could be done with the same approach, as the in this
project, and the experiments with different wind speeds and impact is already completed,
and could be used. One only needs to create the FDS models and analyze the simulations.
Further Work, With a Real-World Perspective
To increase the amount of wood in constructions, in a safely manner, with regards to fire
safety, the following work would, according to the author, be beneficial to carry out:
• Research on how to create generalized values for wood’s thermal properties, so fire
dynamics calculations for wood are not specific or depending on the wood specie.
• Investigation of the contribution from flashover compartments, with internal surfaces
and interior made of wood, regarding HRR and fire spread on facades and to adjacent
buildings.
• Continuous research in fire stops, specifically made for wooden facades.
• Continuous research in fire paint and treatment of wood to decrease its ability to ignite
and contribute to a fire.
• Research on how composite materials, with wood included, can be used beneficial.
• Investigations on how to make it safe to use a whole wooden covered facade in high
risers and buildings, with top floor placed more than 5.1 m above ground [9].
• Develop prescriptive codes, which includes the use of wood in high­rise buildings.
• Collaborative work between urban planners and fire safety experts, which specialize in
wildfires, on how to include fire safety into new urban development. Which can create
a fire safety plan, which not only covers a single building but a larger area.

Calculations related to the facade fire experiments and its fire source 
All theory and values is either from Enclosure Fire Dynamics, by Björn Karlson and James G. Quintiere 
or from the SFPE Handbook, fifth edition. 
It has been decided that the fuel source is methanol (
)
The energy release / burning rate (Q) of methanol
The burning rate is determinedd by equation 3.5 from Enclosure Fire Dynamics
Equation 3.5 
Where:
: Area of the pool of methanol in [m²]
: mass loss rate [kg/m²s]
: combustion efficiency [-]
: Heat of of combustion [MJ/kg]
Normally when calculating the the energy release rate for a pool fire, one should use equation 3.6 too 
K eK
However, acording to the SFPE Handbook p. 866, the diameter effect does exist, hence for methanol 
when D < 0.6 m it is recomended to use 
:

Flame height
The flame height is calculated to make sure, that the flames will not be higher than the hood in the 
FireLab. 
Equation 4.3 from Enclosure Fire Dynamics is used. 
K
:
Where: 
L: Flame height [m]
: The energy release rate [kW]
D: Diameter of the pool, here the pan that contains the methanol [m]
This gives the following flame heights:

K
 = 0.3686153156
Here the flame height varies between 0.3 m to 0.37 m. This is the average flame height over the entire 
course of the methanol fire. Meaning that the flame height will fluctuate over time, and can be both 
higher and lower than the calculated values.
Time to ignition of wood

-The wooden facade is thermally thick. 
ou see this as being two black bodies.

based Codes by Lars Schiøtt Sørensen

The estimated time it takes for the two types of wood to ignite, when exposed for the critical heat flux 
and ignition temperature is relatively 3.78 min (226.8 s) and 6.29 min (377.9 s)

J
Preliminary Calculations for Safety
Distance
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
131
==PAGE:144==
Preliminary Safety Distance Calculations
SFPE Handbook, chapter 86 calculation
For this calculation the two building facades modelled in the up-scaling of the CFD model. Each 
facade have a dimension of width 15 m and height 17 m. The thickness of the facade cladding is 21 
mm, but in FDS due to meshes is 50 mm, and then there is bout horizontal and vertical wooden 
battens each on 50 mm, so the gathered width of combustible facade is 0.15 m
According to the SFPE handbook chaptor 86, the irradiance levels needed to cause iqnition in a 
building, which is exposed, is for spontaneous ignition 33.5 kW/m², and for piloted ignition (such as 
brands), 12.5kW/m². 
The formular for irradiance is 
R
K
e
Where 
K : radiation reduction factor for glazing of exposed building when wall cladding is  
noncombustible. Since the facade cladding is wood and combustible, K=1
So the formula can be rewritten to
which corresponds to the Stefan-Boltzmann equation including the configureation factor. 
Note that as explained in therory section, whene a facade is combustible, the whole facade counts, as
an emitter, as if there were fire on the whole facade.
  
Chapter 86 uses the following table to find the heat release for a 3.7 m tall building 
Table from (Zalosh, R. G. (2014). Appendix A: Flame Radiation Review. https://doi.
org/10.1002/9781118903117.app1)
If the values are convereted to ft=m and Btu/sec=kW, and plotted one gets the following 
:

K
K
So if the building is 17 m tall the heat realse pr width in kW is:
K
The width of the facade is 0.15m, given then the heat realease rate pr. 0.15m is 
Then it possible to use Delichatsios correlation for rectangular wall flames, to determine the 
corresponding flame height (Zalosh, R. G. (2014)). 
The flame height from bottom is 23.94 meters, which is 6.94 meter above the top of the building. To 
account for this being a model extended, other littereature is being compared to this value. It is more 
common to see flames about 5 m above a window opening (see Östman, B., Tsantaridis, L. Fire 
Scenarios for Multi-storey Façades with Emphasis on Full-Scale Testing of Wooden Façades). 
Therefore the heat realese rate are being adjusted, so the height of flames does not increase 22 m.

Ulf Wickström: Flames and Smoke calculation

// This fds simulation/model is made for the master thesis: Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High-Rise Buildings
// Simulating Scenario Sc1.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 5 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
// fds version: FDS6.7.9

// This fds simulation/model is made for the master thesis: Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High-Rise Buildings
// Simulating Scenario Sc3.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 10 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
// fds version: FDS6.7.9

//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

// Simulating Scenario Sc2.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 7.5 m distance between facades.

M
FDS code: High-rise facade fire, fire from
window ignition
168
Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High­Rise Buildings
==PAGE:181==
// This fds simulation/model is made for the master thesis: Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High-Rise Buildings
// Simulating Scenario Sc1.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 5 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
// fds version: FDS6.7.9 
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
//---- MAIN
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
&HEAD 
    CHID='FACADE5' 
    TITLE='Brandspredning mellem to facader' /
&TIME           
    T_BEGIN  =  0
    T_END    =  3600 /        
&MISC           
    RESTART  =  .FALSE.
    TMPA     =  9.1
    BNDF_DEFAULT   =  .FALSE.
    RADIATION      =  .TRUE.
    SURF_DEFAULT   =   'FRAME' /
&RADI           
    TIME_STEP_INCREMENT  =  12
    NUMBER_RADIATION_ANGLES  =  100
    ANGLE_INCREMENT  =  20
/

// This fds simulation/model is made for the master thesis: Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High-Rise Buildings
// Simulating Scenario Sc3.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 10 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
// fds version: FDS6.7.9 
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
//---- MAIN
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
&HEAD 
    CHID='FACADE10' 
    TITLE='Brandspredning mellem to facader' /
&TIME           
    T_BEGIN  =  0
    T_END    =  3600 /     
&MISC           
    RESTART  =  .FALSE.
    TMPA     =  9.1
    BNDF_DEFAULT   =  .FALSE.
    RADIATION      =  .TRUE.
    SURF_DEFAULT   =   'FRAME' /
&RADI           
    TIME_STEP_INCREMENT  =  12
    NUMBER_RADIATION_ANGLES  =  100
    ANGLE_INCREMENT  =  20
/

//---- MESH Total Cells 2052050                                                                                 
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Facader

// This fds simulation/model is made for the master thesis: Thermal Radiation Between Wooden Facades in High-Rise Buildings
// Simulating Scenario Sc2.1, with a wastepaper basket at the bottom of the facade. 7.5 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
// fds version: FDS6.7.9 
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
//---- MAIN
//-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
&HEAD 
    CHID='FACADE75' 
    TITLE='Brandspredning mellem to facader' /
&TIME           
    T_BEGIN  =  0
    T_END    =  3600 /        
&MISC           
    RESTART  =  .FALSE.
    TMPA     =  9.1
    BNDF_DEFAULT   =  .FALSE.
    RADIATION      =  .TRUE.
    SURF_DEFAULT   =   'FRAME' /
&RADI           
    TIME_STEP_INCREMENT  =  12
    NUMBER_RADIATION_ANGLES  =  100
    ANGLE_INCREMENT  =  20
/

/   Q_max   100 kW
&SURF   ID='FIRE' HRRPUA=555.555   RAMP_Q='fireramp'  COLOR=   'RED'  /   
&RAMP   ID='fireramp', T=0.0, F=0.0 /
&RAMP   ID='fireramp', T=86,  F=1.0 /
&RAMP   ID='fireramp', T=1140, F=1.0 /
&RAMP   ID='fireramp', T=1200, F=0.0 /

// Simulating Scenario WALL, with a whole wall on fire, T_flame 1100K or 827°C. 10 m distance between facades.

// Simulating Scenario WALL, with a whole wall on fire, T_flame 1100K or 827°C. 12.2 m distance between facades.

// Simulating Scenario WALL, with a whole wall on fire, T_flame 1100K or 827°C. 25.6 m distance between facades. 
// fds model made by Karoline Sandfeld-Nielsen (s173793)
